A fraudulent leasing agent had broken into vacant units at a building on 79th Street and Paxton Avenue and rented them out to desperate migrant families including Daniela’s.</p><p>When the police officer showed up, the message was blunt: The family had one week to get out.</p><p>Finding safe and suitable housing has become more difficult in Chicago as migrants face precarious legal statuses because of changing federal policies under President Donald Trump's administration.</p><p>Their lack of stable income and fear of deportation have made them more vulnerable to these types of housing scams. They also have to grapple with sky-high rents and an <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/race-class-communities/2024/02/06/chicago-migrants-search-for-housing-as-deadline-looms" target="_blank" ><u>affordable housing</u></a> shortage.</p><p>And a new state law that took effect in January makes it easier for landlords to call the police and vacate squatters without a lengthy eviction process, putting migrant households like Daniela’s at risk for homelessness.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/race-class-communities/2024/02/06/chicago-migrants-search-for-housing-as-deadline-looms" >Chicago migrants are under pressure as the deadline to find permanent housing looms</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d70000" name="image-d70000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Daniela packs up some of her belongings on Feb. 5, 2026, on the evening her family left the apartment on 79th Street and Paxton Avenue in Chicago’s South Shore neighborhood.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Esther Yoon-Ji Kang/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>Searching for a stable home</h3><p>Since arriving in Chicago in 2023, the family has moved more than five times. After staying at a migrant shelter for months, they got a temporary rent assistance voucher from the state and found a house that fit all six members for nearly $2,000 per month.</p><p>But the stability was short-lived.</p><p>Within six months, the temporary voucher payments expired, and with only one of the adults holding a steady job, they couldn’t keep up with rent.</p><p>“During those six months, I went out every day, walking street by street looking for work, and I didn't find anything,” said Daniela’s partner, Pedro, in Spanish.</p><p>Without employment, Daniela said they were <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/immigration/2024/10/07/organizers-try-to-calm-chaos-at-a-south-side-home-depot-where-migrants-look-for-work" target="_blank" ><u>desperate for cash</u></a> and begged in the streets for money with her 4-year-old in tow, even on cold winter days.</p><p>Eventually the family had to leave the spacious home they paid for with the voucher. They doubled up with friends and acquaintances in separate homes until the family figured out how to fit six people in a one-bedroom apartment.</p><p>“My mom, her partner and two other kids usually shared one bedroom," Daniela said. “Me, my partner and child used to sleep in the living room divided by a curtain.”</p><p>They were crammed.</p><p>So in November 2025, when a man who spoke fluent Spanish offered them a two-bedroom apartment for $1,000 — just a little more than what they’ve been paying for their one-bedroom — they jumped at the chance, ignoring several red flags.</p><p>The man, who claimed to be the building manager, never gave them his full name. They never got a lease or receipts when they paid rent in cash. All they had was an informal agreement.</p><p>On top of it all, the apartment was riddled with hazards, including a front door that didn’t lock, no central heat and peeling paint.</p><div Paxton was riddled with hazards, including a front door that didn’t lock, a lack of central heat, and peeling paint.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Esther Yoon-Ji Kang/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Despite the squalid conditions, they called it home. They patched what they could, added a fresh coat of paint and tried to keep it tidy.</p><p>A day before the police showed up, balloons filled the living room for a birthday party they had for one of the kids.</p><p>The party was the last time Daniela and her family said they felt happy and safe in their home.</p><p>According to housing advocates, the family’s search for a place to live reflects that of thousands of migrants in the area.</p><p>“We have seen it plenty of times,” said Michelle Gilbert, legal and policy director at the Law Center for Better Housing.</p><div class="Enhancement" showed up.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Esther Yoon-Ji Kang/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Gilbert also said the new “squatter law” exacerbates low-income renters’ housing struggles because it does not take into account situations where tenants are defrauded by fake leasing agents, like the one Daniela’s family encountered.</p><p>Building owners have a responsibility to make their properties more secure to deter scammers, she added.</p><p>Since the <a class="Link" href="https://www.ilga.gov/Legislation/PublicActs/View/104-0029" target="_blank" ><u>law</u></a> took effect in January, Gilbert said her organization has heard of cases in which police back off once tenants show rent receipts or leases. In those cases, landlords proceed with standard eviction procedures, in which a court hears the circumstances of both sides and decides whether the tenant has a right to stay. A legal process also could provide tenants with access to emergency rental assistance programs.</p><p>Gilbert said new property managers could misuse the squatter law by calling police on tenants they don’t recognize without finding out more information.</p><p>“You can't just buy a building and clear it out by calling the police,” she said.</p><p>Housing advocates also said forcing tenants out so quickly makes them worse off than they were before. In the case of Daniela’s family, they had to leave behind most of their belongings, all their furniture, and even a cart they used to sell empanadas — a source of sorely needed income.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b90000" name="image-b90000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a6b0076/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3742x2100+0+353/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F4c%2F35771da24e2d9a5ced268a79f026%2Fimg-6775.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/156c0a9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3742x2100+0+353/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F4c%2F35771da24e2d9a5ced268a79f026%2Fimg-6775.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9a786e3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3742x2100+0+353/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fa9%2F4c%2F35771da24e2d9a5ced268a79f026%2Fimg-6775.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A building located at 7900 S. Paxton have recently undergone renovations.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Esther Yoon-Ji Kang/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>A lawyer for Atlas Asset Management, the property management arm of the company that owns the building where Daniela’s family was living, said in a statement to WBEZ that upon taking over the building, “our team discovered complex and challenging conditions at the site” and vacated the units in order to “ensure the safety of residents. This included working to identify all authorized tenants with valid leases.”</p><p>Atlas did not answer multiple questions from WBEZ, including how management secured the building upon purchase, how it determined there were squatters, and whether the company worked with any area nonprofits to assist families with relocation.</p><p>A review of records obtained through a WBEZ public records request to the Chicago Police Department shows that officers were called to the building Feb. 2 by a property manager “regarding squatters in 3 or 4 of the vacant units of the building.”</p><p>Public records also show that the building, which had long been in disrepair, was previously owned by the 5812 Group, <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/housing/2025/09/05/chicago-landlords-renters-property-management-llc?subscription=true" target="_blank" ><u>a limited liability company</u></a>. It was sold to Osiris Chicago Portfolio LLC, which shares an address with Atlas, on Nov. 20, 2025 — about three months before Daniela and her family were kicked out.</p><p>Adam Walls, the head of previous owner 5812 Group, did not respond to a request for an interview.</p><p>Despite the building’s conditions, which mirrored those of many properties in that area, desperate migrants have settled in South Shore, which has come to be known as the “eviction capital of Chicago.”</p><p>The neighborhood is also home to the <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/public-safety/2025/10/10/chicago-immigration-raid-south-shore-apartment-donald-trump-ice?subscription=true" target="_blank" ><u>site</u></a> of a federal immigration raid last year. There were also <a class="Link" href="https://www.propublica.org/article/chicago-venezuela-immigration-ice-raid-landlord-tren-de-aragua" target="_blank" ><u>reports</u></a> of fraudulent property managers collecting rent from unwitting migrants in the apartment building where the raid took place.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div He has seen many migrants face housing challenges in recent years and also sympathizes with property managers and landlords, who face rising costs and higher taxes." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/86a80f6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3704x2079+0+350/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F73%2F1f%2Fa35b357a410197c02a6a1ab1e840%2Fimg-6790.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1abaf1c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3704x2079+0+350/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F73%2F1f%2Fa35b357a410197c02a6a1ab1e840%2Fimg-6790.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/86a80f6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3704x2079+0+350/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F73%2F1f%2Fa35b357a410197c02a6a1ab1e840%2Fimg-6790.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chris Amatore, who owns Manage Chicago, said he has seen many migrants face housing challenges in recent years. He also sympathizes with property managers and landlords, who face rising costs and higher taxes.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Esther Yoon-Ji Kang/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>‘Compassion is not a rental system’ </h3><p>As president of Manage Chicago, Chris Amatore oversees thousands of apartments for low-income renters. In late 2023, he provided apartments for hundreds of migrants in his buildings after experiencing a spiritual awakening.</p><p>Since then, Amatore has seen many migrants face housing challenges due to a lack of job opportunities, a language barrier, and “just not knowing where to turn to,” making them easy prey for apartment scams.</p><p>“It’s really just math,” Amatore said. “They can't afford the market rents. It's $2,000 to $2,700 for a three-bed [unit]. Post-COVID rents have gone up faster than a migrant salary.”</p><p>He said credit score requirements and proof of income are also hurdles for migrants seeking to rent apartments. Rising costs, higher taxes and inflation have also made landlords hike rents.</p><p>“I definitely sympathize with landlords, because it's happened to me, and it sucks,” Amatore said. “No one else is going to care. Your bank doesn't care. The utility companies are still gonna turn off the water or gas. Unfortunately, compassion is not a rental system.”</p><p>Still, Amatore believes there’s a humane way to work with these families.</p><p>“What I would do is say, ‘OK, I'm the real landlord…. ‘Let's make a deal.’ [I’d] put them on a plan and give them an opportunity,” he said.</p><p>Lately, Amatore has started noticing there are more migrants sleeping on the streets and in tents.</p><p>“At first, I didn't see a lot, but I do see a lot more migrants under the Canalport bridge and by 18th and Halsted over there, and other areas,” he said.</p><p>As rents rise, aid has run out, and housing options dwindle, he thinks he’ll see more migrants out on the streets and in tents.</p><p>Daniela and her family hope that doesn’t happen to them.</p><p>Shortly after they fled the South Shore apartment they found a new, more expensive one on the West Side for $1,400 a month. But the move once again uprooted the family. It took weeks to enroll the children in their new school. And while more adults in the family are employed now, they still scramble to come up with rent each month.</p><p>Meanwhile, back in South Shore, the apartment Daniela and her family were living in has been repainted and the kitchen remodeled. As of early April, Atlas Asset Management had listed the unit for $1,525.</p><p>The listing read: “Welcome to your new home in the heart of Chicago, IL! This two-bedroom, one-bathroom unit is available now and ready to impress.”<br></p>
entries.author: Adriana Cardona-Maguigad |  WBEZ

entries.title: Review: ‘Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom’ at the Goodman is near perfection
entries.summary: <p>August Wilson was a night owl.</p><p>He'd hang out in bars and diners until the wee hours of the morning, his friend, author and University of Washington professor Charles Johnson told me a few years ago when I was writing a story about Wilson’s 15 years living in Seattle.</p><p>One story from Johnson stood out: One night, at an IHOP around 3 a.m., the duo witnessed an impromptu rap battle that led to a fistfight. Johnson remembers his inclination to flee — but Wilson, cool as cucumber, watched the altercation, then jotted notes.</p><div class="RatingCard Enhancement" data-module data-rating="3.5" data-align-center> <div class="RatingCard-title"> ‘Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom’ </div> <div class="RatingCard-content"> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star"> <span class="RatingCard-star-full"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> <span class="RatingCard-star-half"> <svg class=""> <use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use> </svg> </span> </span> </div> </div><p></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-ba0000" name="module-ba0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><b>When:</b> Through May 3<br><b>Where:</b> The Goodman, 170 N. Dearborn St.<br><b>Tickets:</b> $34+<br><b>Run time:</b> 2 hours and 30 minutes, including a 15-minute intermission<br><b>Info:</b> <a class="Link" href="https://www.goodmantheatre.org/show/ma-raineys-black-bottom/" target="_blank" >goodmantheatre.org</a></div> </div><p>That scene plays in the back of my mind as I watch “Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom,” on stage at the Goodman Theatre until May 3. Wilson was renowned for his way with words and language — a trait many, including his wife, Constanza Romero, who told me this directly, attribute to his tendency to sit in public and people watch. He was able to capture the natural essence of conversation and embed it into the dialogue of his plays. The truth and honesty that result are something to behold.</p><p>Those traits are on full display in “Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom,” which unfolds in 1920s Chicago. Prolific blues singer Ma Rainey has a recording session in a studio. She’s an hour late, but her band is on time. While her manager and the owner of the studio, both of whom are white, impatiently await her arrival — time is money — the band warms up while bantering over philosophical topics like art, race and religion.</p><p>One of those band members is Levee, the ambitious trumpeter. In this production, directed by Chuck Smith, Levee is deftly portrayed with a precise combination of charm, energy and passion by Al’Jaleel McGhee. I’ve seen this production many times, and I don’t believe I’ve seen anyone embody Levee the way McGhee did opening night. This character, and the show, belonged to him.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b50000" name="image-b50000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/65a68c2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5624x3156+0+820/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F29%2Ffa%2F517098a842839b1a0ef492f4399f%2F1-ma-raineys-black-bottom-production-photos.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3cd8c96/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5624x3156+0+820/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F29%2Ffa%2F517098a842839b1a0ef492f4399f%2F1-ma-raineys-black-bottom-production-photos.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bc8ca9a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5624x3156+0+820/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F29%2Ffa%2F517098a842839b1a0ef492f4399f%2F1-ma-raineys-black-bottom-production-photos.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="E. Faye Butler plays the titular role and Al'Jaleel McGhee is Levee in “Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom.”" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bc8ca9a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5624x3156+0+820/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F29%2Ffa%2F517098a842839b1a0ef492f4399f%2F1-ma-raineys-black-bottom-production-photos.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f74851d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5624x3156+0+820/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F29%2Ffa%2F517098a842839b1a0ef492f4399f%2F1-ma-raineys-black-bottom-production-photos.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/bc8ca9a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5624x3156+0+820/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F29%2Ffa%2F517098a842839b1a0ef492f4399f%2F1-ma-raineys-black-bottom-production-photos.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>E. Faye Butler plays the titular role and Al’Jaleel McGhee is Levee in “Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Justin Barbin</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><p>When McGhee, who was also a force in Goodman's recent production “Revolution(s),” first bursts on stage, with his brand new pair of shoes, the excitement and self-assurance in that moment is palpable. These shoes symbolize more than just fancy footwear — this is about his pride and sense of self. They’re an extension of his determination to decide and craft his own destiny, and McGhee nails it.</p><p>When we learn Levee’s backstory and the horrific act he witnessed his mother suffer, McGhee burns like a furnace. His rage was so hot I felt it from my seat. Throughout the show, his presence is magnetic, and as a viewer, it was hard to not focus solely on the immaculate performance taking place.</p><p>Smith clearly has a knack for staging Wilson’s work — and obviously, this is not his first rodeo. Wilson’s writing is lyrical, at times poetic, but Smith keeps the production grounded, and everything happening feels real and raw.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-4c0000" name="image-4c0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1a606d0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7939x4456+0+420/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F60%2F2f%2Febed226c46d2b1f6fe759140fdd6%2F6-ma-raineys-black-bottom-production-photos.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/52aaa04/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7939x4456+0+420/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F60%2F2f%2Febed226c46d2b1f6fe759140fdd6%2F6-ma-raineys-black-bottom-production-photos.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b04044d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7939x4456+0+420/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F60%2F2f%2Febed226c46d2b1f6fe759140fdd6%2F6-ma-raineys-black-bottom-production-photos.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="6_Ma Rainey's Black Bottom Production Photos.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b04044d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7939x4456+0+420/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F60%2F2f%2Febed226c46d2b1f6fe759140fdd6%2F6-ma-raineys-black-bottom-production-photos.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1479607/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7939x4456+0+420/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F60%2F2f%2Febed226c46d2b1f6fe759140fdd6%2F6-ma-raineys-black-bottom-production-photos.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b04044d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/7939x4456+0+420/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F60%2F2f%2Febed226c46d2b1f6fe759140fdd6%2F6-ma-raineys-black-bottom-production-photos.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Cedric Young (Slow Drag) and Al ’ Jaleel McGhee (Levee) perform in “Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Justin Barbin</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>E. Faye Butler is a delight as Ma Rainey. She is a complicated diva, with emphasis on the <i>diva</i>. Her performance at times is larger than life, which the role calls for, but she also poignantly delivers the true meaning of the blues and the genre’s deep connection to Black people. She also understands her place as a Black woman in 1920s America, and the true dynamics at play between herself, her manager and the owner of the studio. She knows she has the upper hand now, and that she must play hardball, because once her voice is on record, and they no longer need her to make money, everything will change.</p><p>Wilson thrives in these complexities and pulls so much humanity out of these characters. His take on the arts and Black artists' relationship to exploitation are poignant in themselves, but he also puts these themes next to religion and Black people's place in Christianity. All the while, the show still maintains its laughs and joy, and of course, there’s music. Harry Lennix, perhaps best known as an actor, serves as music director in this production and makes his presence felt, especially when Ma Rainey performs her titular tune.</p><p>What makes this production feel special is the lightness. It’s still a drama, but between the heavy blows, there’s joy in the sparring. Smith has such comfort with the work that he allows a looseness in the actors' performances that I appreciate. The ending, which I won’t fully spoil here, still lands as hard as ever — with Levee watching his potential future drip like blood from his fingertips. The moment is fully earned, and despite the devastating outcome, the journey leading up to it was an enjoyable ride.</p>
entries.author: Mike Davis | WBEZ

entries.title: Chicago bakers step up their sourdough game by branching out beyond the boule
entries.summary: <p>Of all the breads out there, none offers as much emotional attachment — some might even say obsession — as sourdough to those who make it.</p><p>Bread has long represented more than the sum of its humble parts — from breaking bread as code for connecting with others to bread lines symbolic of hard times. But during the pandemic, homemade sourdough became a way for people to take matters into one’s flour-dusted hands. Once the world returned to some sense of normalcy and businesses reopened, the task of making sourdough at home and the challenges of its slow natural fermentation process were mostly relegated back to the professionals.</p><p>While some people may have abandoned their sourdough projects, others have not only continued to feed their starters but branched out with interesting flavors (even Taylor Swift has embraced her sourdough era with her blueberry lemon and funfetti breads), using sourdough as a vehicle for experimentation and self-expression. Some of these bakers share their creations through Instagram and pop-ups, and one baker who leaned into sourdough during the pandemic has parlayed a successful Instagram business into a brick-and-mortar bakery opening soon.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-bc0000" name="image-bc0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8bf03c9/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5345x3000+0+283/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8b%2F91%2F63c6ce484010b9079f91c7de1979%2Fsourdough-260401-021.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/325b448/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5345x3000+0+283/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8b%2F91%2F63c6ce484010b9079f91c7de1979%2Fsourdough-260401-021.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5694613/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5345x3000+0+283/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8b%2F91%2F63c6ce484010b9079f91c7de1979%2Fsourdough-260401-021.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Fresh sourdough bread lies on the counter at Bad Butter, a new bakery in Bucktown on the Northwest Side, Tuesday, March 24, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5694613/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5345x3000+0+283/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8b%2F91%2F63c6ce484010b9079f91c7de1979%2Fsourdough-260401-021.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/869dc59/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5345x3000+0+283/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8b%2F91%2F63c6ce484010b9079f91c7de1979%2Fsourdough-260401-021.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5694613/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5345x3000+0+283/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8b%2F91%2F63c6ce484010b9079f91c7de1979%2Fsourdough-260401-021.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Fresh sourdough bread lies on the counter at Bad Butter, a new bakery in Bucktown on the Northwest Side, Tuesday, March 24, 2026.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The passage of 2022’s Home-to-Market Act allowed home bakers to register as a cottage food operation, taking their creations out of their home kitchens to customers. One of those bakers was Mirela Hukic.</p><p>For her, baking has always been a form of therapy. It began when her family came to the United States as refugees in the ’90s during the Balkans War. Hukic would often bake bread alongside her mother and even briefly had a small bread-baking business as a tween. That was a distant memory when she became a mother and began a career in finance.</p><p>Everything changed when Hukic got laid off a year and a half ago. That same day, she told her husband she was going to make some sourdough, her family’s favorite bread. “He laughed, and said, ‘You just got laid off from a high-stress job, and you want to make the most complicated bread of all,’” she said. “Once again, baking was so therapeutic for me, and so that's what I did.”</p><p>Word of mouth among the friends Hukic gifted her bread to led to the creation of <a class="Link" href="https://breadcultchicago.com/home/" target="_blank" ><u>Bread Cult</u></a>, an organic sourdough business with pickups available around Chicago at small businesses around the Chicago area; one of the places where her preordered bread can be picked up is at Madame ZuZu’s Emporium in suburban Highland Park, the tea shop founded by Smashing Pumpkins front man Billy Corgan and his wife, Chloe Mendel.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-da0000" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Bread Cult’s original sourdough, made with organic flour, filtered water and ancient sea salt, is still pop-up bakery’s top seller.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Photo by Justin Paris; food styling by Breana Moeller </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Bread Cult is based out of Hukic’s Portage Park home, where she produces some 180 sourdough loaves a week. Varieties include cinnamon raisin, rosemary polenta and dark chocolate pumpkin, along with seasonal items like blueberry lemon (Hukic’s favorite). The takesumi loaf incorporates activated charcoal and hence its all-black hue. One of her twin 10-year-old sons came up with the idea for the popular PB&J loaf, while both hand-decorate all the bags. "Their artwork is a huge part of Bread Cult as most of my customers are families,” says Hukic.</p><p>But Hukic’s original sourdough loaf made with organic flour, filtered water and ancient sea salt is still the top seller. For those who want to give sourdough bread making a try, Hukic sells a portion of her starter, aptly named The Cult Starter.</p><p>Smaller in scope but similar in inspiration is plant-based microbakery Someday Sourdough, the brainchild of Ania Bilinska. Born in Poland, she moved to Chicago when she was a kid. Growing up, she turned to baking “to calm and soothe my soul when things were a bit rocky,” she said.</p><p>In 2023, her remote work dwindled, and Bilinska once again returned to baking. Sourdough became a focus with the encouragement of a fellow bread-baking friend.</p><p>“Sourdough bread taught me that you could make a lot with very little, which has always been my approach to baking and cooking,” Bilinska said. “I didn't know I could make bread out of literally just flour and water and that it could be so delicious, beautiful and fulfilling on an emotional level for the person creating it.”</p><p>Since then, Bilinska, who is vegan, and her starter “Stella” have created a number of sourdough treats via Someday Sourdough, ranging from whole-grain gluten-free sourdough loaves and pumpkin-spiced sourdough brioche to sourdough bagels. Her baked goods are available at her pop-ups (check <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/somedaysourdough/" target="_blank" ><u>her Instagram</u></a> for her schedule). Like Hukic, Bilinska bakes out of her home in Rogers Park.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Someday Sourdough’s plant-based sourdough brioche bun dusted with cardamom rose and coconut sugar, topped with a cherry blossom.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Bilinska, who finds inspiration in nature, farmers markets and unique flavor combinations, initially struggled with figuring out a sourdough method but has found her own style through experimentation. “Once you start doing it yourself, you realize the best way to approach it is to infuse your own personality, schedule and preferences in it,” she said.</p><p>As someone who struggles with health and digestion issues, Bilinska began experimenting with creating a plant-based sourdough brioche dough. From there, she started playing around with different items she could make with the same dough, while simultaneously exploring other substitutions for ingredients that were traditionally used.</p><p>Her brioche dough doughnuts in flavors like espresso martini glazed and lemon Earl Grey have earned a loyal following. They’re made without any seed or refined oils and are sweetened with maple syrup. She fries them in avocado oil. Bilinska is currently trying to create a homemade vegan butter with the possibility of using it for laminated pastries next.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ca44aa0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6149x3451+0+325/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fed%2F53%2F4a3d307a4159a57d3bbb107f0b6f%2Fsourdough-260401-025.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8e65936/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6149x3451+0+325/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fed%2F53%2F4a3d307a4159a57d3bbb107f0b6f%2Fsourdough-260401-025.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ca44aa0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6149x3451+0+325/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fed%2F53%2F4a3d307a4159a57d3bbb107f0b6f%2Fsourdough-260401-025.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“With sourdough, even though it’s technical and precise, you have to adapt every day in order to get the loaf that you’re looking for and that’s both frustrating and rewarding,” says Koester. “Sourdough can take a while to get a handle on it, but it’s something that grabbed a hold of me in a way that nothing else really has.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>When Dan Koester was laid off from his head baker job at Soho House Chicago during the pandemic, he became one of the many who turned to sourdough for solace, albeit with skills that provided a head start. He began selling loaves on Instagram first as Dan the Baker and later as <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/badbutterchicago/" target="_blank" ><u>Bad Butter</u></a>.</p><p>“With sourdough, even though it’s technical and precise, you have to adapt every day in order to get the loaf that you’re looking for and that’s both frustrating and rewarding,” said Koester. “Sourdough can take a while to get a handle on it, but it’s something that grabbed a hold of me in a way that nothing else really has.”</p><p>When Koester works with new bakers, he describes the sourdough process as a lot of repetition, understanding the peculiarities of your starter and the importance of checking the dough’s temperature, which can fluctuate throughout the process, especially vital for proofing times. “I’ve touched this dough 300 or so times so you just build that memory and muscle of what it should feel like.”</p><p>For the last three years, Koester has been selling his available-by-preorder baked goods — with many of them incorporating sourdough — at the Emily Hotel, where he makes them in a spare kitchen. His sourdough loaves have ranged from blue masa corn and oat to benne seed and beer. Equally creative is his sourdough focaccia with past favorites like black garlic and Parmesan, loaded baked potato, and potato and dill. Koester’s bagels and English muffins are made with sourdough too. All his croissants — including butter, ham and cheese, chocolate and a special Chicago hot dog — feature a small portion of his sourdough starter for added flavor.</p><p>This month, Koester is fulfilling his dream of opening his own bakery when <a class="Link" href="https://badbutterchicago.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Bad Butter</u></a> opens at 1655 W. Cortland St. in Bucktown. Formerly Mable’s Table, his new bakery will offer coffee drinks and eventually sandwiches on his homemade bread.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5e5bfcc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3022x1696+0+1040/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F29%2Fe6%2Fb38e0c684d2492c40673acab819c%2Fimg-2870.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Divs Ray from Umami from Scratch makes a vegan version of sfiha (a meat flatbread from theLevantine region) with spiced smoked tofu.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>For Divs Ray, who grew up in India and moved to Chicago in 2015 for school, baking was a way to create treats that she liked but wasn’t finding elsewhere, including ones without nuts or tree nuts or too much sugar. They also needed to be vegetarian. </p><p>During the pandemic, she began recording her home baking adventures first under her personal Instagram and later as <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/umamifromscratch/" target="_blank" ><u>Umami From Scratch</u></a>, referencing the complex flavors in her baked goods, including spices like cardamom, clove and sumac.</p><p>“The focus is 100% on highlighting flavors and techniques from the old spice routes and translating that in a modern baking context,” she said. “All the ingredients and flavor profiles are going to be one of a kind.”</p><p>Since then, Ray has made many pop-up appearances, including her first at Side Practice Coffee where she introduced her black garlic miso butter sourdough focaccia. Madeleines have become a specialty in flavors like saffron orange blossom, jasmine dark chocolate and kimchi Parmesan. She recently made 1,000 of them for a wedding all out of her home kitchen.</p><p>Divs also makes a variety of bread, ranging from Hokkaido-style milk bread and challah to Bombay chile cheese lava buns. She uses sourdough for some of her breads, including focaccia and sfiha, a Middle Eastern flatbread that she tops with smoked tofu.</p><p>“If I have to pick one thing that I'm most passionate about it is bread,” she said, calling it her one true love. “Whenever I feel very emotional, overwhelmed or sad, to process my emotions I make bread.”</p>
entries.author: Lisa Shames | For the Sun-Times

entries.title: 5 noteworthy art gallery exhibitions to check out during Expo Chicago
entries.summary: <p>By far the biggest recurring event on Chicago’s contemporary art schedule is Expo Chicago, an annual, high-end art fair at Navy Pier that draws thousands of art collectors and aficionados from across the region and beyond.</p><p>This year’s edition, which will take place April 9-12 and feature more than 130 galleries from around the world, will be accompanied by an array of complementary events at museums, galleries and other art spaces across the city.</p><p>Many of the city’s dozens of commercial galleries take care to schedule exhibitions during this week that they hope will show off their spaces and lure museum groups and other art lovers who are in town for Expo.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/arts-and-culture/2026/04/03/expo-chicago-art-fair-obama-center-contemporary-galleries-navy-pier-tickets" >Expo Chicago art fair returns with ‘refined’ floor plan, Obama Center ties</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/art/2025/10/16/roger-brown-collection-imagist-exhibit-sheboygan-wisconsin" >‘Incredibly important’ Imagist Roger Brown gave his collection to Chicago. How’d it end up in Wisconsin?</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/art/2025/12/30/chicago-imagists-60th-anniversary-art-institute" >As the 60th anniversary of the Chicago Imagists approaches, the artists have never enjoyed more success</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>That is certainly the case with Gray on the Near West Side, one of the city’s oldest and most respected galleries. “Of course, we want to have a strong exhibition on view and do what we can to put our best foot forward,” said Valerie Carberry, Gray’s president and chief executive officer.</p><p>In November, Gray began representing the estate of Roger Brown, one of the best known of the Chicago Imagists who died in 1997, and it will present its first exhibition devoted to the artist during Expo.</p><p>“There’s something really relevant about his work,” Carberry said, “that’s reverberating not just with collectors today who are new to Roger Brown but, also, you can see things expressed in contemporary art — his sense of patterning and the clarity of his compositions.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>In November, the Gray gallery began representing the estate of Roger Brown, one of the best known of the Chicago Imagists who died in 1997, and it will present its first exhibition devoted to the artist during Expo Chicago.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The show, titled “Weathervane,” opened March 19 and runs through June 13. It features 11 of Brown’s paintings from the 1980s and ’90s that depict a tense relationship between the built environment and natural world, with many including looming storms and other dramatic weather phenomena.</p><p>Along with the strong patterns and color contrasts in these semi-abstracted compositions, which can be read from afar, are often diminutive figures and vehicles that require viewers to lean in. </p><p>“So, they really reward that close inspection,” Carberry said.</p><p>Here’s a look at four other noteworthy, diverse shows taking place around Chicago during Expo:</p><h3>Marshall Brown, <b>‘</b>Palaces and Prisons,<b>’</b> <a class="Link" href="https://westernexhibitions.com/" target="_blank" >Western Exhibitions</a>, 1709 W. Chicago Ave., through May 30</h3><p>Marshall<b> </b>Brown, an architecture professor at Princeton University in New Jersey, uses the medium of collage to create often large-scale works that conjure what Scott Speh calls “unconventional and almost impossible structures and worlds.”</p><p>The owner of Western Exhibitions in West Town discovered the architect and futurist during the decade or so he taught at the Illinois Institute of Technology and became excited to show his creations.</p><p>“I’ve always been interested in artists who create their own worlds or who look at the world and try to envision it as a different place,” Speh said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-0c0000" name="image-0c0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" </figure> </div> </div><p>Brown, who was showcased in 2022-23 at the Santa Barbara Museum of Art in California, has had four previous exhibitions at the gallery. The first in 2013, “Center of the World, Chicago” included three newsreel-like films with different speculative futures for the Windy City.</p><p>This two-part show offers continuations of two series, “Prisons of Industry” and “Prisons of Invention.” The latter is inspired by “Carceri d’invenzione<b><i>,</i></b>”<b><i> </i></b>a celebrated series of 18th-century etchings by Giovanni Battista Piranesi depicting intricate architectural fantasies based on Roman buildings.</p><h3>Jessica Diamond, Hélène Fauquet and Jason Hirata, <b>‘</b>Ethical Managers Make Their Own Rules,<b>’</b> <a class="Link" href="https://www.bodenrader.com/" target="_blank" >Bodenrader</a>, 1620 W. Carroll Ave., through May 2</h3><p>For the 2 1⁄2-year-old Near West Side gallery’s exhibition during Expo, owner Ethan Kennemer has brought together the work of three related yet still contrasting conceptual artists from different generations.</p><p>He first settled on the language-based work of Diamond, the best known of the three. She emerged in the 1980s in New York’s downtown art scene and was featured in a long-running solo exhibition that ended in 2025 at the Hirshhorn Museum and Sculpture Garden in Washington, D.C. Joining her will be Fauquet, a French artist who had her first solo show in the United States in 2024, and Jason Hirata, who explores the nature of authorship in his work.</p><p>“I think the show gets at a core concern that I’m always thinking about as gallery owner, which is how exhibitions function within themselves and how artists approach exhibition-making, which I think is always interesting and something that is sometimes taken for granted or overlooked,” Kennemer said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a Paulina St., April 8 through May 23</h3><p>Vintage cinematic images of Cairo blur with childhood memories in Nabil’s hand-colored silver prints — dreamy, nostalgic imaginings of his native city.</p><p>“His work engages with longing, memory and return, and it’s very cinematic but also emotive,” said Emma McKee, director of Ibrahim in West Town.</p><p>This show, the busy artist’s inaugural outing at Ibrahim, follows a small solo exhibition that closed in January at the Los Angeles County Museum of Art and precedes another that opens in May at the Musée d’Orsay in Paris.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a50000" name="image-a50000"></a> <picture Gallery leaders were eager to showcase Nabil during that time, in part because he returns the gallery to its photographic roots and offers works that deal with both personal and collective histories.</p><p>On view will be more than 15 photographs and three videos, including a 2015 one, “I Saved My Belly Dancer,” which features famed movie actress Salma Hayek, whose father is of Lebanese descent.</p><h3>B. Ingrid Olson, <b>‘</b>All Lock No Key,<b>’</b> <a class="Link" href="corbettvsdempsey.com" target="_blank" >Corbett vs. Dempsey</a>, 2156 W. Fulton St., through April 25</h3><p>Corbett vs. Dempsey co-owner John Corbett calls Olson a “hometown hero” because the Chicago native and former intern at the gallery has achieved considerable success, including her inclusion in the prestigious Whitney Biennial in 2024.</p><p>She will be featured at the Near West Side gallery during Expo in what Corbett said would be Olson’s first solo show in Chicago since early in her career. It includes 40 works from the tiny to the monumental, including photo-based and found-object wall works.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“All Lock No Key” by B. Ingrid Olson</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Photo by Robert Chase Heishman for Bob</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>At the center of the site-specific exhibition is an installation that re-creates a room in the gallery called The Vault, where it typically presents video and film works, with Olson offering a kind of turned-out version of the distinctive space she has long admired.</p><p>“The work deals with the body, the kind of ambiguity of perception and how one’s own body responds to seeing a body represented. So, she is really interested in the notion of proprioception, which is how a body through the senses is aware of its own position in space,” Corbett said.</p>

entries.title: What are Chicago area lighthouses used for?

data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="JohnGach.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f911dd0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1302x731+0+295/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F23%2Fb9%2F046a1f694a17ba307b0f45df8ad7%2Fjohngach.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c59cbe5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1302x731+0+295/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F23%2Fb9%2F046a1f694a17ba307b0f45df8ad7%2Fjohngach.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f911dd0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1302x731+0+295/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F23%2Fb9%2F046a1f694a17ba307b0f45df8ad7%2Fjohngach.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A photo of John Gach during his Coast Guard boot camp days.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of John Gach</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>For several months in 1976, John Gach had a unique job.</p><p>“I’m the only person I know that has ever been a lighthouse keeper,” says Gach, who is now 70 and lives in Woodstock, Illinois.</p><p>Park Ridge resident Wayne Barton, 88, was also a part of the exclusive club more than a decade earlier in the late 1950s. He says people called them “wickies,” because in the early days, lighthouse keepers relied on oil lamps to produce a sustainable glow, requiring them to routinely trim the lamps’ wicks.</p><p>But Barton and Gach aren’t that old. By the time the Coast Guard posted them at the Chicago Harbor Lighthouse, it was more a flick of the switch. The <a class="Link" href="https://www.lighthousefriends.com/light.asp?ID=234" target="_blank" ><u>structure was electrified</u></a> in the 1920s.</p><p>Still, Barton says between duties like light maintenance, weather monitoring and night shifts, the role was serious and deliberate.</p><p>“You always had a routine. If you weren’t fixing this, you were checking that,” he says. “You can’t make mistakes.”</p><p>While Gach says his posting had creature comforts that his much earlier predecessors could have never dreamed of, like a small color television, there was one major downside: the foghorn. Try sleeping through that for 96 hours straight in bad weather.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-060000" name="image-060000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8f60c6e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1950x1094+0+710/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcb%2F97%2F73f12df04daaaf88ec615b3fe605%2Fyoungwaynebarton.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8900c3e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1950x1094+0+710/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcb%2F97%2F73f12df04daaaf88ec615b3fe605%2Fyoungwaynebarton.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/da80be6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1950x1094+0+710/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcb%2F97%2F73f12df04daaaf88ec615b3fe605%2Fyoungwaynebarton.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="YoungWayneBarton.JPG" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/da80be6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1950x1094+0+710/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcb%2F97%2F73f12df04daaaf88ec615b3fe605%2Fyoungwaynebarton.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d5341f1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1950x1094+0+710/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcb%2F97%2F73f12df04daaaf88ec615b3fe605%2Fyoungwaynebarton.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/da80be6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1950x1094+0+710/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fcb%2F97%2F73f12df04daaaf88ec615b3fe605%2Fyoungwaynebarton.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A portrait of Wayne Barton when he served in the Coast Guard in the 1950s.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Wayne Barton</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“I don’t remember the frequency of how many times it rang per hour…. You start to fall asleep, there it is again,” Gach says. “It was tough, but it had to be done.”</p><p>There was a real sense of responsibility — mariners and boaters still relied on them, after all.</p><p>Neither foresaw their positions eventually becoming obsolete, but Barton and Gach were among the last generations to man lighthouses. Chicago Harbor’s signal was automated in 1979.</p><p>As navigational technology continued to progress, the singular role of the lighthouse shifted, and the accompanying stations that humans once sustained became redundant. But many of these structures still exist, in varying forms, both as ongoing aids to navigation and as maritime legacies preserved for historical value.</p><h3><b>Historical significance to crumbling structures</b></h3><p>Nationwide, a little more than half of the 1,500 lighthouses originally constructed remain, according to Jeff Gales of the nonprofit <a class="Link" href="https://uslhs.org/about/Staff-and-Volunteers" target="_blank" ><u>United States Lighthouse Society</u></a>.</p><p>“For a very long time, the Coast Guard was lessening the role of human beings at lighthouses because modern technology was taking over,” he says. “And, you know, they don’t want to waste money.”</p><p>Although historically, keepers were necessary to illuminate lighthouses, by the 1960s and ‘70s, automation had largely spread across the country. As stations became unmanned, physical conditions often deteriorated and some were decommissioned.</p><p>Still, Gales says today, the Coast Guard maintains many of the actual light signals for safety.</p><p>“Having a tangible, visible, physical lighthouse that you can reference is still very important,” he says. “They’re definitely something that are used by mariners and … technically could be a back-up if a system fails.”</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://www.ibiblio.org/lighthouse/il.htm" target="_blank" ><u>Waukegan Harbor Lighthouse</u></a> just north of Chicago, for example, is still an active aid to navigation. But all that remains is an <a class="Link" href="https://www.lighthousefriends.com/light.asp?ID=238" target="_blank" ><u>unadorned 35-foot cast-iron cylinder</u></a> with a green flash atop.</p><p>Further down the coast, in contrast, is the photogenic 153-year-old Grosse Point Lighthouse in Evanston. It’s an early example of a lighthouse being decommissioned and transferred from federal to local authority, an undertaking the city of Evanston began in 1935.</p><p>“Everything you see up here is original,” says Don Terras on a recent tour of the 113-foot tower, highlighting an antique clockwork mechanism in the watch room that controlled the flash.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f40000" name="image-f40000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/6281cd6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3866x2170+0+378/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7b%2F1a%2Fa4c1bbc64c96b8fead95b1976cd9%2Fdonterrasgrossepoint.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fb3f1e2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3866x2170+0+378/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7b%2F1a%2Fa4c1bbc64c96b8fead95b1976cd9%2Fdonterrasgrossepoint.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="DonTerrasGrossePoint.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fb3f1e2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3866x2170+0+378/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7b%2F1a%2Fa4c1bbc64c96b8fead95b1976cd9%2Fdonterrasgrossepoint.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a78a2d1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3866x2170+0+378/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7b%2F1a%2Fa4c1bbc64c96b8fead95b1976cd9%2Fdonterrasgrossepoint.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fb3f1e2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3866x2170+0+378/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7b%2F1a%2Fa4c1bbc64c96b8fead95b1976cd9%2Fdonterrasgrossepoint.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Don Terras has overseen the Grosse Point Lighthouse for more than four decades.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Katie Riordan</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Terras, the longtime caretaker of the property and head of the Lighthouse Park District, also points out the lantern’s immense, second-order Fresnel lens.</p><p>“It’s the last one that operates on the Great Lakes,” he says.</p><p>The tower still functions as a private aid to navigation, and its surrounding beach area is a popular place to recreate. The <a class="Link" href="http://www.grossepointlighthouse.net/" target="_blank" ><u>facility’s museum</u></a>, open during the weekends in the summer months, helps explain the direct role that lighthouses played in facilitating local growth and development.</p><p>Terras says it can be hard to picture today, but in the latter half of the 19th century, the area’s lakefront teemed with commercial maritime traffic.</p><p>“During many years in the 1800s, Chicago surpassed New York, San Francisco, New Orleans, all the major seaports, in arrivals and departures,” he says. “Chicago’s port, of course, because of winter, was only eight months long. The other ports had 12-month seasons.”</p><p>Each lighthouse's distinct flash pattern helped all these ships and schooners safely navigate, and in the case of Grosse Point, to also warn of shallow waters or shoals.</p><p>Illinois’ lighthouses were a link, Terras says, to safely get material and people to the Midwest and beyond.</p><h3><b>Restoring a beacon</b></h3><p>The most prominent link on that chain is one you’ve probably walked by — one undergoing a push to preserve its cultural significance.</p><p>The Chicago Harbor Lighthouse is hidden in plain sight, resting in Lake Michigan at the extremity of the northern breakwater, about half a mile from the end of Navy Pier.</p><p>“Our biggest concern is that no one knows why it’s there or what it’s there for,” says Kurt Lentsch, who started the nonprofit <a class="Link" href="https://savethelighthouse.org/" target="_blank" ><u>Friends of the Chicago Harbor Lighthouse</u></a> to bring it back to life. “It’s been in the background of our memories for our generation, for 100-plus years.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-7c0000" name="image-7c0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ab32c9a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F39%2F6e%2Ffe89d3c1438cb62d603c9697be32%2Flighthouse-08xx25-4.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7755b4a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F39%2F6e%2Ffe89d3c1438cb62d603c9697be32%2Flighthouse-08xx25-4.jpg class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The deteriorating Chicago Harbor Lighthouse seen from the air, Tuesday, Aug. 19, 2025. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The lighthouse was originally built in 1893 against the backdrop of the World’s Columbian Exposition. In 1917, officials decided to move it to its current location.</p><p>The Coast Guard operates the actual light as an active aid to navigation, but the station is in a state of disrepair. According to an <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2019/12/8/20976289/chicago-harbor-lighthouse-redevelopment-david-morton-scott-gidwitz-theaster-gates-lake-michigan" target="_blank" ><u>assessment report</u></a>, it’s rusted, crumbling and gutted.</p><p>Lentsch, a longtime recreational boater, was spurred to action in 2022 amid worries that the property could be put up for <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2020/11/1/21542197/lighthouse-lake-michigan-chicago-ownership-feds-want-it-back-national-park-service-gsa" target="_blank" ><u>auction</u></a>, potentially ending up in private hands.</p><p>He was determined to keep the historic landmark public. His volunteer organization is awaiting final approval to take over the property from the city of Chicago to repurpose it as an accessible visitor center. The group envisions it as an extension of the nearby Loop’s Museum Campus — a tourist attraction with a major focus on local education.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div programming out at the light — from a lighthouse perspective, from engineering, to weather, to water sampling,” Lentsch says.</p><p>The pending stewardship change is possible through the federal government’s<a class="Link" href="https://www.nps.gov/orgs/1220/national-historic-lighthouse-preservation-act-nhlpa.htm" target="_blank" ><u> National Historic Lighthouse Preservation Act of 2000</u></a>, which has allowed local entities and nonprofits to commit to saving outdated light stations across the country.</p><p>Lentsch says helping the public better understand the area’s rich maritime heritage is a major reason why people want to save these beacons.</p><p>“It’s such an iconic part of the skyline,” he says. “It’s been there before most of those buildings were Turn around, look at the city of Chicago and love the view that I loved,” Gach says.</p><p>Barton hopes visitors will take away an appreciation for those who literally lit the path to Chicago.</p><p>“There’s only a handful of people [that have] ever been on a lighthouse, let alone work it,” he says. “It was special.”</p><p><i>Katie Riordan is a journalist based in class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Allison Lavey and Beth Anne Scharle sit with their children Zach and Ava.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Katie Riordan</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div>Allison Lavey, her wife Beth Anne Scharle and their two children, Zach, 5, and Ava, 4, live in Evanston. Allison is an elementary school teacher and Beth Anne is a primary care physician. They like to listen to Curious City as a family.<br><br>They also frequently visit Lake Michigan’s Lighthouse Beach, in the shadow of the Grosse Point light tower. The historic lighthouse is emblematic of Evanston to them and has long captured their imaginations.<br><br>“Lighthouses are unique,” Lavey says. “There’s also sort of like a mystique about them … I’m interested in the ways that communities have embraced local lighthouses, whether or not they’re functional.”<br><br>Zach already knows that lighthouses prevent shipwrecks, but the family was curious about other lighthouses along Chicago’s lakefront.<br><br>“Are any of them still in use in the traditional sense?” Lavey asked. “If not, what else are lighthouses used for these days?”<br><br>As an educator, Lavey is a proponent of asking questions and looks forward to sharing what she learns with her fourth-grade students.<br></div> </div><p></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-750000" name="html-embed-module-750000"></a><script async src="https://modules.wearehearken.com/wbez/embed/4190.js"></script> </div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Katie Riordan

entries.title: Boy, 16, gunned down outside his South Shore school during drive-by
entries.summary: <p>A drive-by shooting in the South Shore neighborhood left a 16-year-old boy dead outside his own school on Tuesday morning. </p><p>Kenyae Franklin, 16, was seen running south on the 7800 block of South Coles Avenue when three gunmen exited a white Range Rover, ran toward the victim, and began shooting about 9:15 a.m., according to a police report and the Cook County medical examiner's office.</p><p>After the attack, the three jumped back into the Range Rover and sped away. </p><p>Franklin, a student at Safe Achieve Academy, 7877 S. Coles Ave., was taken to the University of Chicago Medical Center, where he was pronounced dead at 9:43 a.m., the medical examiner's office said. </p><p>Police recovered several rifle rounds and 9mm rounds, according to the report which said the Range Rover was later found in an alley, set on fire, in the 7800 block of South Paxton Street. </p><p>No arrests have been made. </p><p>Area 2 detectives are investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: After Trump's Iran threats, Pritzker calls for ouster

href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-d/" target="_blank" >Trump talk stokes anger, fear even as US and Iran OK ceasefire</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-h/" target="_blank" >Violet Miller</a>, <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-k/" target="_blank" >Mary Norkol</a>, and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-u/" target="_blank" >Tina Sfondeles</a></h5><p><b>Little relief</b>: As President Donald Trump’s threat Tuesday to destroy Iran <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-o/" target="_blank" >gave way to a ceasefire between the two countries and Israel</a>, Chicagoans with ties to Iran said they felt little relief. Earlier, Trump’s words drew criticism from Republicans and Democrats, including <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-b/" target="_blank" >Gov. JB Pritzker, who called for the president’s ouster</a> under the 25th Amendment.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Trump had threatened that a "whole civilization will die tonight" if Iran failed to reach a deal that included reopening the Strait of Hormuz, the crucial waterway where about a fifth of the world’s oil is transported under Iranian supervision. A two-week ceasefire agreement was reached about 90 minutes before the deadline, but <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-n/" target="_blank" >attacks in Iran and Gulf Arab countries resumed Wednesday</a>, throwing the deal into question, the Associated Press reported. Even before the new strikes were reported, much about the agreement was unclear, as the sides presented different visions of the terms.</p><p><b>'Psychological warfare'</b>: Mehrnoush Soroush, a University of Chicago professor from Iran, has gotten used to Trump walking back threats to her home country. Still, those threats are their own form of violence, she said. "It’s psychological warfare because you’re doing this to millions of people worldwide; it’s not just Iranians," said Soroush. "Everywhere in the Middle East, they’re all freaking out."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-p/" target="_blank" ><b>READ target="_blank" >Migrants face scams, sky-high rents and squalor in search for housing</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-m/" target="_blank" >Esther Yoon-Ji Kang</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-q/" target="_blank" >Adriana Cardona-Maguigad</a></h5><p><b>The challenges</b>: Finding safe and affordable housing has become more difficult in Chicago as migrants face a precarious legal status because of changing policies under President Trump’s administration. Lack of stable income and fear of deportation have also made many migrants more vulnerable to housing scams. And a new state law has made it easier for landlords to call police and evict squatters without a lengthy process.</p><p><b>One family's story</b>: A Venezuelan family who migrated to Chicago in 2023 shared with WBEZ their story of being scammed by a fraudulent leasing agent. The agent had broken into vacant units and rented them out to desperate migrant families. Police eventually arrived and gave them one week to vacate the building.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-a/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p> </p><h4>GANGS ✶</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-280000" name="image-280000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/273144b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5813x3262+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1e%2F70%2Fd48a9fe54f6987adc8da5cc0e690%2Fhoover-260408-22.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/648c184/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5813x3262+0+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1e%2F70%2Fd48a9fe54f6987adc8da5cc0e690%2Fhoover-260408-22.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0688b21/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5813x3262+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1e%2F70%2Fd48a9fe54f6987adc8da5cc0e690%2Fhoover-260408-22.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="John Gleeson, center, walks with Justin A. Moore, left, attorney for Larry Hoover, after a clemency hearing Tuesday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-f/" target="_blank" >Larry Hoover’s wife and an ex-NY mob prosecutor both urge clemency</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-z/" target="_blank" >Frank Main</a></h5><p><b>Clemency push</b>: John Gleeson, a former New York mob prosecutor, stood up Tuesday for Larry Hoover, once the king of the Gangster Disciples for decades in Chicago. Gleeson said he prosecuted criminals similar to Hoover, but that Hoover was different. He’s "grown up" and is remorseful, Gleeson told the Illinois Prisoner Review Board. The board is considering the 75-year-old ex-gang leader’s request for clemency from Gov. JB Pritzker.</p><p><b>The hearing</b>: Dozens of people, including Hoover’s wife, Winndye, attended a prisoner review board hearing downtown, where a panel heard testimony from Hoover’s supporters, along with prosecutors who oppose his freedom. Prosecutors said granting clemency would ignore the seriousness of the killing for which he’s locked up, would minimize crimes he later committed in prison and would put the public in danger.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Hoover and his supporters have increasing hope for his release despite decades of rejections in the state’s separate parole process. That’s because Hoover won a separate grant of clemency last year from President Trump for a federal drug conspiracy sentence.</p><p><a class="Link"
