appoint Keith Pettigrew as CEO, though the meeting's agenda made no mention of the motion. Housing advocates are calling foul.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-w/" target="_blank" ><b>Remembering Rachel Gansner</b></a>: A mom who started an all-girls baseball league at Warren Park on the North Side, Gansner died Saturday at age 45.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-yd/" target="_blank" ><b>New Baggu</b></a>: After opening its first Midwest store in Wicker Park in 2024, the California-based bag and accessories brand plans to expand its Chicago footprint with a second spot in Lincoln Park.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-yh/" target="_blank" ><b>Sound & Gravity returns</b></a>: More than 50 artists, including Resavoir, Dos Santos and Kassa Overall, will perform across seven venues for the second edition of the multivenue festival near Avondale.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-yk/" target="_blank" ><b>3.5 stars for ‘Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom’</b></a>: Powerhouse performances make the production feel real and raw in the remarkable staging of the August Wilson classic, writes Mike Davis.</li></ul><hr><p></p><h4>MUST-READ COMMENTARY 🗣️</h4><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 20px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-71fdd6d9-334f-11f1-a9fc-19b6c7c3cd0e"><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-yu/" target="_blank" ><b>Neil Steinberg</b></a>: The artist formerly known as Kanye West is facing the music over his ill-fated embrace of Nazism. He says he regrets that decision now, but his "Heil Hitler" song was bound to bite him.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-jl/" target="_blank" ><b>Liza Khalil and Rev. Ara Torosian</b></a>: The U.S. welcomed us when we fled persecution. Now it's closing its doors.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-jr/" target="_blank" ><b>Rich Miller</b></a>: Illinoisans are pushing state leaders for more affordable housing options.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-jy/" target="_blank" ><b>Letter to the editor</b></a>: Housing near public transit encourages car-free living, writes Cyrus Dowlatshahi of Bucktown.</li></ul><hr><p></p><h4>ON WBEZ 91.5 FM 📻</h4><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-jj/" target="_blank" ><b>In the Loop with Sasha-Ann Simons, 9 a.m.</b></a></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 0px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-71fdfde8-334f-11f1-a9fc-19b6c7c3cd0e"><li><b>Betting on the future</b>: How prediction markets work and why the Trump administration is suing Illinois over regulating them. Guests Noah Henderson of Loyola University, Karl Lockhart of DePaul University Law School and Democratic state Sen. Michael Hastings (19th District) explain.<br></li><li><b>Expo Chicago returns</b>: The Sun-Times' Ambar Colón explains some of the art fair's changes this year. Artist Faheem Majeed, of the Floating Museum, discusses local art scene representation amid federal funding cuts.<br></li></ul><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-jt/" target="_blank" ><b>Say More with Mary Dixon and Patrick Smith, 10 a.m.</b></a></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 0px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-71fe24f2-334f-11f1-a9fc-19b6c7c3cd0e"><li><b>Middle East conflict</b>: Ali Tarokh, an Iranian refugee, Aaron Hughes of About Face: Veterans Against the War and Congressman Bill Foster (D-IL) talk about the U.S. and Israeli war on Iran. Callers weigh in.</li></ul><hr><p></p><h4>FROM THE PRESS BOX ⚾🏀🏒</h4><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 20px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-71fe24f5-334f-11f1-a9fc-19b6c7c3cd0e"><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-jh/" target="_blank" ><b>Cade Horton’s season ends</b></a>: The Cubs' right-hander will undergo elbow surgery.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-jk/" target="_blank" ><b>Reese trade analysis</b></a>: With Angel Reese shipped off to Atlanta, the Sky can target a new star free agency. The problem is, they don’t have a compelling pitch for why the best players should join them, writes Alissa Hirsh.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-ju/" target="_blank" ><b>Donovan faces the media</b></a>: Bulls coach Billy Donovan on Tuesday held his first meeting with the media since vice president of basketball operations Arturas Karnisovas and general manager Marc Eversley were fired 24 hours earlier.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-tl/" target="_blank" ><b>Real talk for Hawks</b></a>: The Blackhawks need to be logical and buy out Andre Burakovsky this summer, writes Ben class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Helena Kim shows off one of her restored vintage Coach purses in her home studio.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-tj/" target="_blank" >Chicago content creator finds healing through the art of restoring vintage Coach bags</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-tt/" target="_blank" >Esther Bergdahl</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-ti/" target="_blank" >Minju Park</a></h5><p>Helena Kim's compact West Loop home studio, measuring about 12 by 12 feet, keeps a place for every tool. Her closets conceal an eye-popping variety of glues, dyes, conditioners, thread and other supplies. Floor-to-ceiling shelves boast the fruits of her labor: vintage Coach handbags, luxury purses rescued from thrift shops and restored to their original blues, greens, rich browns and chic blacks.</p><p>"I do have a very, very large collection at this point," Kim admitted. "It was nothing I set out to do. But every time I went thrifting, I’m like, 'I need to rescue her! She needs help!'"</p><p>On social media, Kim is known by her nickname, Yoonie, which is derived from her Korean name; she has more than 1.2 million followers. In her soothing videos, she explains how to hunt for and rehabilitate the saddest of bargain bin finds. She prefers distressed and abandoned small leather goods, which she revives with saddle soap, conditioners and a jaw-dropping assortment of trade skills.</p><p>When Kim stitches together loose panels, she’s gauging the tensile response of the leather. After she suds up her horsehair brush, she listens for the scrape of its bristles against the bag’s surface. As she massages a thirsty purse with lotion, her hands remember caring for her weary parents after work.</p><p>"When I do really resonate with a bag," Kim said, "I’m just emotionally connected to a bag, or I find this need to tell their story, or a story develops in my head." She loves finding clues about past lives in thrifted purses: A set of bridal hair clips, a magazine clipping to bring to a hairdresser.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-td/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p> </p><h4>WATCH: CHICAGO’S COACH RESCUER ▶️</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="200" height="113" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/TM8No4SGtkk?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Restoring vintage Coach bags is more than Yoonie's hobby, it's like 'therapy'"></iframe> </div> </div></div><hr><p></p><h4>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h4><p>Yesterday, we asked you: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-tu/" target="_blank" ><b>What’s a clear sign of spring in Chicago?</b></a></p><p>Here’s some of what you said…</p><p>"Full tour boats." — <i>Sue Wynn</i></p><p>"Tulips come up on Michigan Avenue." — <i>Tara Goodwin</i></p><p>"Baseball fans complaining about the bullpen and lack of offense." — <i>Vince LiFonti</i></p><p>"Automobiles with tickets for not moving the vehicle during assigned street cleaning." — <i>Edward M. Bury</i></p><p>"When the weather gets off the roller coaster and stays in the upper 60s for more than three days." — <i>Scott Wisniewski</i></p><p>"The return of the piping plovers to Montrose Beach Sanctuary signals abundant insects, warm sunshine for tiny feathered bodies and nature's symphony ready for another beautiful season!" — <i>Toni Scavo</i></p><p>"Potholes, of course." — <i>Christopher Vaughn</i></p><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated by</b>: Matt Moore<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia</p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you. Become a member to make stories like these free and available to everyone. Learn more at </i><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wiydre-l-il/" target="_blank" ><i>suntimes.com/member</i></a><i>.</i></p>

entries.author: Kristen Schorsch | WBEZ

entries.title: Wheaton eyes ban on sale of delta-8 THC, ‘gas station heroin’ and other products
entries.summary: <p>Wheaton is poised to join an increasing number of suburban towns in banning the sale of certain psychoactive products that are typically available at gas stations and vape shops.</p><p>The city has drafted an ordinance prohibiting the sale of products containing substances such as delta-8 tetrahydrocannabinol, or THC.</p><p>The Food and Drug Administration in 2024 <a class="Link" href="https://www.fda.gov/news-events/press-announcements/fda-ftc-continue-joint-effort-protect-consumers-against-companies-illegally-selling-copycat-delta-8#:~:text=In%20June%202022%2C%20the%20FDA,consumed%20delta%2D8%20THC%20products" target="_blank" >warned</a> of several companies selling delta-8 THC products that closely resemble popular snack food brands “by using similar brand names, logos, or pictures on packaging that consumers, especially children, may confuse with traditional foods.”</p><p>Wheaton has also moved to ban the sale of kratom, recreational nitrous oxide and tianeptine products.</p><p>According to the FDA, <a class="Link" href="https://www.fda.gov/drugs/drug-alerts-and-statements/fda-warns-consumers-not-purchase-or-use-any-tianeptine-product-due-serious-risks?sm_guid=NzU4Mjk3fDYzMDQ4NTE3fC0xfHBpZWRtb250Y2FyZUBiZWxsc291dGgubmV0fDcxMDA4OTF8fDB8MHwyMTc4MzczNzZ8MTA4NnwwfDB8fDc1MjQzNnww0" target="_blank" >tianeptine</a> has been referred to as “gas station heroin” with product names including Tianaa, Zaza, Neptune’s Fix, Pegasus and TD Red. It’s not approved by the FDA for any medical use.</p><p>For more on this story, please visit <a class="Link" href="https://www.dailyherald.com/20260407/news/wheaton-eyes-ban-on-sale-of-delta-8-thc-gas-station-heroin-and-other-products/" target="_blank" >DailyHerald.com</a>.</p>

entries.title: South Shore school boarded up after student, 16, gunned down
entries.summary: <p>The front doors of a South Shore school were boarded up after a student was gunned down outside the building Tuesday morning. </p><p>Plywood covered the doors, shattered glass lined the sidewalk, and bullet holes marked the walls of Safe Achieve Academy, 7877 S. Coles Ave. on Wednesday where 16-year-old<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/07/one-dead-in-south-shore-drive-by" target="_blank" > Kenyae Franklin</a> was shot to death. </p><p>Franklin was seen running south on the 7800 block of South Coles Avenue when three gunmen exited a white Range Rover, ran toward the victim, and began shooting about 9:15 a.m., Chicago police said. The teen was shot outside the front doors of the school. </p><p>After the attack, the three jumped back into the Range Rover and sped away.</p><p>Franklin was taken to the University of Chicago Medical Center, where he was pronounced dead, police said. </p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" school on lockdown after the shooting to protect students and staff. </p><p>"Our first priority, every day, is the safety and well-being of our students and staff. Our thoughts are with the family and friends of our student during this very difficult time," the school said. </p><p>Police recovered several rifle rounds and 9mm rounds, according to the report which said the Range Rover was later found in an alley, set on fire, in the 7800 block of South Paxton Street.</p><p>No arrests have been made.</p>

entries.summary: <p>Let’s go to the theater — together! Just in time for spring culture season, WBEZ/Chicago Sun-Times is launching a Theater Club on April 18 with a trip to see “White Rooster” at Lookingglass Theater. We’ll see the play, chat as a club with some members of the artistic team and socialize with each other over the arts.</p><div class="Enhancement FormEnhancement" data-align-center> <div data-ajax-endpoint="https://sfmc-subscribe.wbez.org/v1/subscription" class="Form-newsletter-form" method="POST" > <div class="Form-newsletter-item"> <div class="EmailInput"><label type="submit">Sign Up </button> </div> <div class="Form-items-item"> <input type="hidden" name="acquisition_campaign" value="ARTS_Affordance"> </div> <div class="Form-newsletter-disclaimer"> <p>By subscribing, you agree with WBEZ’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/terms-of-use" target="_blank" >Terms of Use</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/privacy-policy" target="_blank" >Privacy Policy</a></p> </div> </form> </bsp-form> </div> </div><p>Tickets for our Theater Club outing to "White Rooster" will go on sale on Wednesday 4/8 at noon. Tickets will be available through links in our social media bio on <a class="Link" href="https://www.tiktok.com/@wbez_chicago" target="_blank" ><u>TikTok</u></a>. WBEZ High Fidelity Members will receive a special invite by email.</p><p>Our idea for a Theater Club started when our arts reporters noticed how many people attend shows solo, or are arts curious but not sure where to start. We all attend plenty of shows alone, but know theater is more fun with a friend. Going with a friend – or, let’s say, a club of 30 new friends! – can make trying something new less daunting. And that’s our goal: We want to help more of you explore Chicago’s eclectic and enterprising theater scene.</p><p>After testing a pilot last fall, we are announcing a three-event series in April, May and June that will feature trips to three world premiere plays at three different area theaters. We’ll kick off in April at Lookingglass. May will see our group headed to Steppenwolf for the world premiere of “Windfall,” by Academy Award-winner Tarell Alvin McCraney. And in June, we are headed to see “The Targeted,” a new play being staged by A Red Orchid Theatre at the Chopin Theatre.</p><p>Artistically, Chicago is known as a gutsy theater town where new work still happens. So it’s fitting that our theater club members will be experiencing a trio of new American plays that are still being shaped even as we view them. And that’s part of the excitement about our adventure: Not only do Theater Club events help stoke audience enthusiasm for the arts and foster a community of culture goers, but we get to be part of early audiences who are reacting to these works for the first time.</p><p>Tickets for "Windfall" and "The Targeted" will be released closer to the show dates.</p><p>Follow us on TikTok for more updates on future Theater Club outings! Until then, follow along with our theater coverage on <a class="Link" href="http://wbez.org" target="_blank" ><u>wbez.org</u></a> or via our theater reviews on <a class="Link" href="http://suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><u>suntimes.com</u></a> and our <a class="Link" href="https://www.tiktok.com/@wbez_chicago" target="_blank" ><u>WBEZ account on TikTok.</u></a></p>
entries.author: WBEZ Staff

entries.title: Johnson picks new deputy mayor of community safety, transportation commissioner, and seven more new hires
entries.summary: <p>Mayor Brandon Johnson announced a big round of new hires Wednesday morning, including a replacement for Garien Gatewood, who was fired last month from his post as deputy mayor for community safety. </p><p>Johnson also announced that William Cheaks, Jr. will serve as commissioner of the Chicago Department of Transportation, an appointment that requires City Council approval. Craig Turner has been <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2025/07/25/transportation-department-craig-turner-mayor-brandon-johnson" target="_blank" >serving as acting commissioner</a> since last summer. </p><p>The rest of the new hires are positions in the mayor's office. </p><p>Emmanuel Andre is the new deputy mayor for community safety. A news release from the mayor's office calls him a "leader in Chicago's restorative justice movement." </p><p>Andre is an attorney who most recently served as a senior member of the Cook County Public Defender’s Office. While there, he built support systems for young people navigating the court system and focused on the ones "most impacted by systemic harm," according to the release. Andre also helped expand Cook County's restorative justice community courts and co-founded Circles and Ciphers, a nonprofit focused on restorative justice and community-based approaches to safety. </p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/19/mayor-johnson-deputy-mayor-of-public-safety-garien-gatewood-firing-toxic-work-environment" target="_blank" >Gatewood was abruptly fired</a> from the post last month. He had been serving in the role since Johnson took office and created the job in May 2023. One of Gatewood’s top deputies, Manuel Whitfield, director of violence prevention and community safety, was also fired.</p><p>Gatewood oversaw the community safety plan Johnson credits with historic drops in violent crime. He was fired amid allegations of a hostile work environment and self-promotion. Before he was fired, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/city-hall/2026/03/24/brandon-johnson-garien-gatewood-firing-inspector-general-deborah-witzburg-complaint" target="_blank" >Gatewood filed a formal complaint</a> against top mayoral aides with the city inspector general’s office in October, accusing them of interference in City Hall's contracting process. </p><p>Cheaks, Johnson's pick to lead the city's transportation department, is a City Hall veteran with more than four decades of experience "managing large-scale infrastructure and public operations," according to the mayor's news release. </p><p>He most recently served as managing deputy commissioner at the Department of Water Management. He oversaw a workforce of 1,800 employees, a $1.58 billion budget and led efforts to modernize the department and improve accountability and workplace safety. </p><p>Before that, he spent over a decade at CDOT as the deputy commissioner of infrastructure management and in-house construction. A lifelong Chicagoan, Cheaks began his career as a laborer, according to the release. </p><p>Turner was elevated to serve as CDOT's acting commissioner last July. He previously served as the department's first deputy commissioner for over two years. Before that, he led the department’s electrical operations division.</p><p>Johnson announced seven other new hires on Tuesday: <br></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-b22e0cd1-3362-11f1-9d96-f5b42bf31a3f"><li>Jonah Anderson will now serve in a dual role as first deputy mayor for health and human services and director of the mayor’s office of homelessness. Within the mayor's office, Anderson has helped lead Johnson's homelessness strategy and was the primary point of contact for Health and Human Services on the office of homelessness. Sendy Soto recently left her role as the city's first-ever chief homelessness officer. The mayor's office said her two-year role was a temporary one supported by a grant.<br></li><li>Max Budovitch is now the deputy mayor for business, economic, and neighborhood development (BEND). Budovitch previously was the first deputy for BEND where he worked with eight city departments and sister agencies relating to planning, housing, business affairs, consumer protection, economic development, tourism, cultural affairs and special events, according to the mayor's office. <br></li><li>Brian Tyler replaces Budovitch as the first deputy for BEND. He previously was BEND's managing deputy. <br></li><li>Militza M. Pagán will now be the deputy mayor for labor relations. Pagán previously served as the first deputy of labor relations since April 2023.<br></li><li>Marissa Arrez becomes the deputy chief of external affairs. She previously served as first deputy for the mayor’s office of immigrant, migrant, and refugee rights. <br></li><li>Joshua Smyser-De Leon was hired as the deputy director of City Council intergovernmental affairs. He previously worked as the communications director for Ald. Daniel La Spata (1st) and was a government affairs manager at Comcast. <br></li><li>Allison Novelo was hired as a press secretary for Johnson's office. She previously worked in communications for Cook County government. She also was a reporter for CBS News and the Sun-Times.</li></ul>
entries.author: Kaitlin Washburn

entries.title: Cesa al fuego brinda poco alivio de la 'guerra psicológica' a estadounidenses de origen iraní en Chicago
entries.summary: <p>Como la amenaza del presidente Donald Trump de destruir Irán dio paso a un alto el fuego el martes, los habitantes de Chicago con vínculos con el país dijeron que sintieron poco alivio. Anteriormente, las palabras de Trump provocaron críticas de republicanos y demócratas, incluido el gobernador JB Pritzker, quien pidió la destitución del presidente bajo la Enmienda 25.</p><p>Trump había amenazado con que "una civilización entera morirá esta noche" si Irán no llegaba a un acuerdo que incluyera la reapertura del estrecho de Ormuz. Los dos países alcanzaron un acuerdo de alto el fuego aproximadamente 90 minutos antes de la fecha límite.</p><p>Muchos detalles sobre el alto el fuego del martes permanecen poco claros, aunque se suponía que abriría la vía marítima crucial por la que se transporta cerca de una quinta parte del petróleo del mundo bajo la supervisión iraní.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-100000" name="module-100000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Mehrnoush Soroush, profesora de la Universidad de Chicago quien es originaria de Irán, se ha acostumbrado a que Trump retroceda en amenazas hacia su país natal. Sin embargo, esas amenazas son una forma de violencia en sí mismas, dijo.</p><p>"Es una guerra psicológica porque esto lo estás haciendo a millones de personas en todo el mundo, no sólo a iraníes", comentó Soroush, cuya investigación se centra en la antropología de la región. "En todas partes del Medio Oriente, todos están asustados... es parte de la escena bélica".</p><p>Justo antes de la fecha límite impuesta por Trump, Negar Zadh se reunió con un grupo de manifestantes en el Loop para condenar la guerra contra Irán. La residente de Chicago de 32 años dejó Irán hace aproximadamente una década, y no ha podido hablar con su padre en tres semanas.</p><p>"Como iraní, me considero privilegiada si el único sufrimiento de esta guerra para mí son los precios del gas que suben o no poder concentrarme en el trabajo", destacó Zadh. "No sé si mis seres queridos están vivos. No he vivido una vida normal en meses... Estoy funcionado como una persona paralizada".</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-09efb2" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Mehrnoush Soroush, profesora de la Universidad de Chicago, se ha acostumbrado a que el presidente Trump retroceda en sus amenazas contra su país natal, Irán. Sin embargo, dice que las amenazas violentas persisten.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Pritzker y la congresista Marjorie Taylor Greene piden la destitución de Trump</p><p>Antes de que se anunciara el alto el fuego, la retórica de Trump provocó una gran indignación. Algunos pidieron la invocación de la Enmienda 25, que permitiría al vicepresidente JD Vance y a una mayoría del gabinete transferir "los poderes y deberes de un presidente incapacitado" al vicepresidente, según la Biblioteca del Congreso.</p><p>Pritzker, quien es visto como un posible candidato presidencial para 2028, respondió a la última amenaza de Trump llamándolo "un hombre desequilibrado que amenaza con aniquilar a un país entero".</p><p>"Ya es hora", escribió Pritzker en las redes sociales. "Debe invocarse la Enmienda 25 ".</p><p>Pritzker se unió a una alianza inesperada de conservadores que piden la destitución del presidente, incluyendo a la excongresista Marjorie Taylor Greene, al exaliado de MAGA (“Make America Great Again”) y locutor de radio conservador Alex Jones, y a la comentarista conservadora Candace Owens. El pódcaster Tucker Carlson también está pidiendo al gabinete de Trump que rechace cualquier plan que lleve a la muerte de ciudadanos iraníes.</p><p>Ni Vance ni ningún otro miembro del gabinete de Trump ha expresado apoyo para destituir a Trump, por lo que las probabilidades son poco probables.</p><p>‘La democracia no se puede construir sobre escombros’</p><p>En una protesta el martes, Sepidh Sanie, una mujer de 36 años de Chicago y cofundadora del grupo Iran Transition Advocacy, dijo que su peor pesadilla era que la guerra terminara con el régimen aún en el poder. Dijo que el régimen hará cualquier cosa para mantener el control del país, incluso si eso significa sacrificar a su gente.</p><p>“Será una masacre”, comentó Sanie, refiriéndose a la contundente represión del régimen contra los manifestantes. “Con un alto al fuego, no sabemos si eso significa más ejecuciones o más arrestos de civiles. Es una situación en la que todos pierden”.</p><p>Mientras Sanie decía que Trump inicialmente presentó los ataques como un mensaje de esperanza, los iraníes estadounidenses lo vieron claramente, pero mantuvieron silencio para ayudar a amplificar las voces de los iraníes que aún están en el país y que están "desesperados" por ayuda.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Negar Zadh habla con reporteros el martes en el Loop, justo antes de que Irán y Estados Unidos acordaran un alto el fuego de dos semanas. La mujer de 32 años no ha podido comunicarse con su padre, que vive en Teherán, durante tres semanas.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Zadh estuvo de acuerdo y se preocupó de que la guerra dañara la lucha del movimiento de liberación por la libertad.</p><p>“Un ataque militar nunca ha traído liberación a nadie, especialmente con la administración actual sin cumplir con [la ley internacional]”, dijo. “Está claro para mí que el objetivo nunca ha sido ayudar al pueblo de mi país… La democracia no se puede construir sobre escombros”.</p><p>Para Soroush, la profesora de la Universidad de Chicago, ver la respuesta generalizada a la amenaza de Trump reemplazó su ira por orgullo por el pueblo de su país.</p><p>"La civilización y cultura de fuerza [iraní] está completamente en oposición a lo que Trump estaba tratando de destruir", dijo. "Esto es lo que no puede matar".</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Tina Sfondeles

entries.title: White Sox option Opening Day starter Shane Smith to Triple-A Charlotte
entries.summary: <p>That “sense of urgency” described by manager Will Venable on Tuesday in addressing Shane Smith’s early ineffectiveness was set into action Wednesday morning, when the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" >White Sox</a> announced they had optioned the 2025 All-Star pitcher to Triple-A Charlotte.</p><p>In three starts, the All-Star was 0-2 with a 10.80 ERA, walking nine and striking out 11 in in just 8 1/3 innings. He lasted just an inning and two-thirds in a season-opening 14-2 loss in Milwaukee, three innings in a 10-0 loss to the Marlins in Miami on April, and labored through 3 2/3 innings in a 4-2 loss to the Orioles on Tuesday, when he struck out eight but walked five and hit a batter, throwing 99 pitches.</p><p>“Just killing our bullpen,’’ <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/04/07/white-sox-starter-shane-smith-cant-get-through-fourth-admittedly-killing-our-bullpen" target="_blank" >Smith said</a>. “As we get into the season and the games start stacking up, we need these guys to be as fresh as possible. Not being able to get past three and two-thirds in your first [three] starts just puts you in a tough spot.’’</p><p>It's the second consecutive season the Sox sent their Opening Day starter to Charlotte. Sean Burke, the 2025 Opening Day starter, was optioned to Charlotte in August after a season of struggles.</p><p>There were definite signs of progress for Smith. While he still struggled with his fastball command, Smith induced 17 swings and misses.</p><p>To take his place on the roster, the Sox promoted left-handed swingman Tyler Schweitzer from Charlotte and transferred Brooks Baldwin to the 60-day injured list.</p><p>Schweitzer, 25, is 0-0 with a 1.80 ERA in two relief appearances with Charlotte this season, allowing five hits with no walks and four strikeouts. He has gone 22-15 with a 4.13 ERA (158 ER/344.0 IP) and 342 strikeouts in 77 games (57 starts) over five minor-league seasons in the White Sox organization.</p><div class="PageListL" data-module entries.author: Gordon Edes

entries.title: Venta de boletos para el museo del Centro Presidencial Obama comienza el 6 de mayo
entries.summary: <p>Las entradas para el museo del Centro Presidencial Obama estarán disponibles pronto, anunciaron las autoridades el martes.</p><p>El público en general podrá comprar entradas para el museo a partir del 6 de mayo, según un portavoz de la Fundación Obama. Las entradas para los miembros fundadores saldrán a la venta a partir del 21 de abril.</p><p>Los residentes de Illinois tendrán entrada gratis los martes. Todas las entradas al museo son con horario programado, y se recomienda encarecidamente a los visitantes que reserven sus entradas en línea con anticipación.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-130000" name="module-130000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Esta ronda de ventas de entradas abarcará del 19 de junio al 30 de noviembre. Las entradas para fechas futuras se anunciarán con antelación. Inscríbase en <a class="Link" href="http://obama.org/" target="_blank" >obama.org</a> para estar informado cuando se dispongan de futuras fechas.</p><p>Las entradas de admisión general proporcionan acceso a los cuatro niveles del museo, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/04/07/replica-de-la-oficina-oval-del-centro-presidencial-obama-en-chicago-esta-casi-lista" target="_blank" >incluyendo una réplica de la Oficina Oval</a>.</p><p>Los visitantes de 12 años o más pagarán $30 por entrada, mientras que los niños de 3 a 11 años pagarán $23. Los niños menores de 2 años entran gratis.</p><p>La mayor parte del campus del centro presidencial será accesible gratuitamente y abrirá al público el 19 de junio. Esto incluye los jardines, senderos para caminar, un parque infantil, una sucursal de las Bibliotecas Públicas de Chicago (CPL), 28 obras de arte recién comisionadas y más.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Estudiantes de CPS ya pueden acceder a muchos servicios de la biblioteca con una ID estudiantil
entries.summary: <p>Los estudiantes de las Escuelas Públicas de Chicago (CPS) ya pueden acceder a libros, tutores y otros recursos en las bibliotecas de la ciudad sólo con su número de identificación estudiantil.</p><p>Es el último esfuerzo de CPS y de la Biblioteca Pública de Chicago (CPL) para ampliar el acceso a los materiales bibliotecarios a través de <a class="Link" href="https://www.chipublib.org/the-81-club/" target="_blank" >el 81 Club</a>, iniciativa que facilita a los estudiantes obtener libros y otros recursos en las 81 sucursales de bibliotecas públicas.</p><p>Todos los estudiantes de CPS están inscritos automáticamente en el 81 Club, que se lanzó en 2022, y ahora pueden usar su identificación para retirar materiales de la biblioteca, ya sea en persona o en línea “sin necesidad de solicitar, de hacer trámites, ni de pasos adicionales”, <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicago.gov/city/en/depts/mayor/press_room/press_releases/2026/april/81-club-expansion.html" target="_blank" >según CPS y CPL</a>. Anteriormente, los estudiantes debían recoger las tarjetas del 81 Club en persona en una biblioteca.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-c80000" name="module-c80000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Sin embargo, algunos estudiantes pueden querer solicitar una tarjeta de biblioteca tradicional, ya que les permite retirar más de cinco artículos físicos a la vez.</p><p>Funcionarios de ambas agencias dijeron que esta expansión está destinada a ayudar a los jóvenes a aprovechar lo que el sistema de bibliotecas tiene para ofrecer, incluso si carecen de vivienda estable o de un estatus migratorio legal en los Estados Unidos, barreras que podrían dificultar su acceso a la biblioteca en persona o requerir trámites adicionales.</p><p>“Todos sabemos muy bien que el talento está igualmente distribuido en esta gran ciudad en cada uno de sus vecindarios, pero el acceso a las oportunidades no lo está”, dijo Michael Fassnacht, presidente de la junta de la Fundación de la Biblioteca Pública de Chicago, durante una conferencia de prensa el martes en la Biblioteca de la Sucursal de Austin.</p><p>A través del programa, los estudiantes tienen acceso a las bases de datos en línea de la biblioteca y a medios digitales y pueden solicitar servicios de tutoría uno a uno en línea o en persona, entre otros servicios.</p><p>El año pasado, casi 3,000 estudiantes de 390 escuelas diferentes de CPS activaron sus cuentas del 81 Club, <a class="Link" href="https://empowereverychicagoan.org/" target="_blank" >según la Fundación de la Biblioteca de Chicago</a>, aunque eso representa sólo una pequeña parte de los 316,000 estudiantes del distrito. No está claro de inmediato cuántos estudiantes de CPS están utilizando el programa actualmente.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-a557d5" name="image-a557d5"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/af69e4c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe5%2Fad%2F70ae397648948c589e7b9638a5e5%2Fcpslibrarycard-260408-11.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/83ab7f0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe5%2Fad%2F70ae397648948c589e7b9638a5e5%2Fcpslibrarycard-260408-11.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Damarious Jefferson, estudiante de último año de secundaria en CPS, dijo que utilizó el programa 81 Club para trabajar en proyectos, estudiar y pedir prestados libros.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Damarious Jefferson, estudiante de último año en la Austin College and Career Academy, dijo que utilizó el programa para profundizar su interés en las finanzas, que desea estudiar después de la secundaria en la Universidad del Norte de Illinois.</p><p>“Me ayudó con proyectos, a estudiar, incluso a leer un libro a veces”, dijo Jefferson, añadiendo que le gustó tanto que animó a sus amigos a que lo usaran también. “Les dije que es gratis para cualquiera y que deberían usarlo para estudiar, investigar o incluso para su propio trabajo”.</p><p>Los maestros también pueden participar en el programa. Este les otorga acceso a bases de datos de investigación, herramientas de enseñanza, así como eBooks y audiolibros que pueden utilizar en el salón de clases.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: Davey Lopes, a Dodgers infield fixture and record-setting base stealer, dies at 80
entries.summary: <p>LOS ANGELES — Davey Lopes, a member of the Los Angeles Dodgers' record-setting infield of the 1970s and '80s and one of baseball's premier base stealers, died Wednesday. He was 80.</p><p>The Dodgers were informed of his death in Rhode Island by his former wife, Lin Lopes.</p><p>Lopes was a four-time All-Star during his 10 years with the Dodgers. He played in four World Series, winning the 1981 championship. He holds the franchise record for most games played at second base with 1,134. His 1,145 games batting leadoff are second in the organization only to Maury Wills (1,279).</p><p>Lopes was 27 years old when he made his MLB debut on Sept. 22, 1972.</p><p>The next season, Steve Garvey, Bill Russell, Ron Cey and Lopes began the first of 8½ consecutive years starting together in the infield.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">The Dodgers mourn the loss of Davey Lopes, who passed away today at age 80. Lopes was a member of the team’s record-setting infield of the 1970s and 1980s and one of the finest basestealers in MLB history. Our condolences go out to his family and friends. <a href="https://t.co/dJkOk0CWbP">pic.twitter.com/dJkOk0CWbP</a></p>— Los Angeles Dodgers (@Dodgers) <a href="https://twitter.com/Dodgers/status/2041949445349077317?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 8, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Lopes established himself as one of the most prolific base stealers in baseball. He stole 418 bases as a Dodger, second-highest career total in franchise history behind Wills (490). Lopes holds the franchise record with an 83.1% career success rate (minimum 100 steals).</p><p>On Aug. 4, 1974, Lopes became the first Dodger since Wills to steal four bases in a game, and 20 days later, he tied the NL record with five steals against the Cardinals. In 1975, Lopes recorded a then-MLB record 28 consecutive steals without being caught.</p><p>He led the majors in 1975 with 77 steals and the National League in 1976 with 63. In 1978, he stole 45 bases in 49 attempts.</p><p>In 1978, Lopes had the best World Series of his career, starting with two home runs in Game 1 against the New York Yankees. He also won a Gold Glove that season.</p><p>After leaving the Dodgers, he played for the Oakland Athletics (1982-84), the Cubs (1984-86) and Houston Astros (1986-87). He stole 557 career bases — 26th in MLB history — while hitting .263 in 1,812 regular-season games with 155 home runs, 614 RBI, 232 doubles and 50 triples.</p><p>After his playing days, Lopes managed the Milwaukee Brewers from 2000-02. He coached with the Orioles, Padres, Nationals, Phillies and Dodgers.</p><p>He won a second World Series as the Phillies' first-base coach before returning to the Dodgers as the baserunning and first-base coach from 2011-15. He spent his final two seasons in the majors as the Nationals first base coach in 2016-17.</p><p>Lopes is survived by his brothers, Patrick and John, and sisters, Jean, Judith, Mary and Nina.</p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Man found unresponsive on Blue Line CTA train dies
entries.summary: <p>A 53-year-old man died after being found on a Blue Line CTA train early Wednesday on the Near West Side. </p><p>Joel Biloche was found unresponsive about 12:15 a.m. at the Racine station, 430 S. Racine Ave., according to the Cook County medical examiner's office and police.</p><p>Biloche was taken in critical condition to a local hospital, where he was pronounced dead at 1:10 a.m. according to a Chicago Fire Department spokesperson and the medical examiner's office. </p><p>Police said the incident is possibly narcotics-related. </p><p>An autopsy was inconclusive and pending further studies, according to the medical examiner's office. </p><p>The CTA did not respond immediately to requests for comment. </p>
entries.author: Zoe Singer

entries.title: How can Chicago’s top prosecutor hold ICE accountable?
entries.author: Sun-Times

entries.title: CPS Board calls on Gov. Pritzker to reject federal program that funds private school tuition
entries.summary: <p>After a lively debate, the Chicago Board of Education approved a resolution Wednesday calling on Gov. JB Pritzker to reject a federal program championed by the Trump administration that can help cover private school tuition or offset public school costs.</p><p>Though the vote passed 15-0, three members abstained and one board member argued that the resolution was “politically motivated” and a vote should be postponed indefinitely, highlighting the contentious nature of an issue that has divided local and national lawmakers.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.cpsboe.org/content/documents/april_8__2026_arc_public_agenda_to_post.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>The resolution</u></a> calls on Pritzker and state lawmakers to reject any programs that would create, expand or “otherwise reintroduce school voucher programs in Illinois” because they would “undermine Illinois’ commitment to fully funded public schools.”</p><p>The resolution doesn’t mention a specific initiative by name, but it points to renewed efforts by the Trump administration at the federal level which seek to “expand and incentivize voucher or publicly funded scholarships for private schools.”</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/2026/01/28/pritzker-still-deciding-if-illinois-will-join-federal-program-that-funds-private-school-scholarships" target="_blank" ><u>Pritzker is currently weighing whether to opt into</u></a> a new federal program that will allow donors to certain scholarship organizations to reduce how much they owe in federal income tax by up to $1,700. States have until Jan. 1, 2027 to join.</p><p>Those scholarships could fund private school tuition, or unlike other similar programs, they can <a class="Link" href="https://www.chalkbeat.org/2025/12/09/federal-tax-credit-scholarships-enacted-by-trump-start-to-take-shape/" target="_blank" ><u>support activities that cost money at public schools</u></a> like tutoring, after-school programs or school supplies. It was <a class="Link" href="https://www.chalkbeat.org/2025/07/03/how-trump-big-beautiful-bill-will-affect-children-and-schools/" target="_blank" ><u>created last year</u></a> as part of the president’s sweeping tax and spending law.</p><p>Families earning up to 300% of their area’s median income – in Chicago that would be about $230,000 or less – are eligible to receive scholarships. The tax credits won’t be available until next year.</p><p>Support for the program has mostly been divided along political lines. President Donald Trump’s administration and Republicans in Congress have lauded the program as a way to expand education options and make them more affordable.</p><p>Critics say it will undermine public schools by diverting taxpayer dollars. They also charge that public funds will go to schools with discriminatory policies. Private schools, for example, are not required to serve students with disabilities in the same way as public schools.</p><p>The resolution notes that private schools “are not held to the same transparency, accountability, civil rights, or governance standards as public schools.”</p><p>Ellen Rosenfeld, one of the three board members who abstained, led the effort to postpone the vote indefinitely. She said that though she wouldn’t support anything that divests funds from public schools, “politics don’t belong in the board room.”</p><p>Rosenfeld said the matter should instead be on the school board’s legislative agenda, which sets the district’s priorities in Springfield. She urged members to lobby the governor to oppose the program because it is ultimately his decision, and said the board should focus on matters that directly affect Chicago Public Schools students.</p><p>“I want to talk about learning, student voice, chronic absenteeism, student safety, that’s what I want to talk about here,” Rosenfeld said. “I will never support a resolution that is simply politically motivated, it is not our job.”</p><p>But member Debby Pope, who spearheaded the resolution, said it was important to take a stand against the program put forward by the Trump administration, given its moves to <a class="Link" href="https://www.edweek.org/policy-politics/trump-again-proposes-major-education-cuts-in-new-budget-proposal/2026/04" target="_blank" ><u>undermine public education</u></a> and advance policies that have harmed CPS students, such as the president’s <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/02/12/no-recess-lockdowns-less-learning-how-immigration-agents-disrupted-cps-schools-this-fall" target="_blank" ><u>mass deportation campaign</u></a>.</p><p>“How can we even consider the idea of not being opposed to a program that is being proposed by this president with his intentions to destroy the Department of Education?” Pope said.</p><p>Board member Che “Rhymefest” Smith, who also abstained, said the board spends too much time talking about politics and not enough time talking about how to improve student experiences.</p><p>“Fifty-four percent of our time is wasted on politics, and government things, and I’m not saying politics ain’t important, but what are we doing as a board when we only spend 14% of our time talking about student outcomes?” Smith said.</p><p>Member Jitu Brown said he disagreed with that line of thinking and argued “everything is about politics.”</p><p>Therese Boyle, the third board member who abstained, said she needed more time to research the “ins and outs” of the program.</p><p>Ahead of the vote, Chicago Teachers Union Vice President Jackson Potter called for the board to approve the resolution, likening the federal scholarship program to a “fox entering a hen house” that will lead to less accountability for public dollars.</p><p>“Vouchers do more to support wealthy families and children and undermine public services and schools,” Potter said. He pointed to states like Arizona, <a class="Link" href="https://www.propublica.org/article/arizona-school-vouchers-esa-private-schools" target="_blank" ><u>where reporting has found</u></a> students in richer ZIP codes are using vouchers more than those who live in low-income areas, and Florida, where <a class="Link" href="https://hechingerreport.org/universal-vouchers-have-public-schools-worried-about-something-new-market-share/" target="_blank" ><u>public schools have trimmed budgets</u></a> due to low enrollment partly because of the growing popularity of school vouchers.</p><p>Pritzker hasn’t been a strict opponent of similar programs in the past. When running for reelection in 2022 he said he supported the state’s now-defunct Invest in Kids program in a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2022/10/18/23409566/19-questions-candidates-illinois-governor-pritzker-bailey-schluter-wbez-suntimes-issues" target="_blank" ><u>Sun-Times questionnaire.</u></a> Earlier this year, Pritzker told WBEZ he was evaluating the new federal program through a “lens focused on affordability for working families and what best supports Illinois, families and public schools.”</p><p>But he has also raised red flags about how the latest federal policy could be used, <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagotribune.com/2026/03/23/pritzker-mendoza-split-school-tax-credit/" target="_blank" ><u>according to reporting by the Chicago Tribune</u></a>. Earlier this year Pritzker sent Comptroller Susana Mendoza a text message after <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagotribune.com/2026/02/18/opinion-illinois-opt-in-federal-scholarship-program/" target="_blank" ><u>she wrote an opinion piece</u></a> in the Tribune calling for Illinois to opt in.</p><p>“State/federal tax credit dollars would go to support schools that teach children that gay people are evil, that ‘the KKK was fighting against the decline of morality,’ that white supremacy is God’s will — and other crazy notions,” Pritzker’s message said, according to texts obtained by the Tribune.</p><p>So far 29 states, mostly led by Republican governors, have opted into the federal program, according to a <a class="Link" href="https://www.edweek.org/policy-politics/federal-private-school-choice-which-states-are-opting-in/2025/08" target="_blank" ><u>tally kept by Education Week</u></a>. <a class="Link" href="https://www.the74million.org/article/colorado-gov-jared-polis-on-why-hes-taking-trumps-free-money/" target="_blank" ><u>Only two Democratic governors</u></a> have opted in, but a few others who initially said they were opposed to the <a class="Link" href="https://www.edweek.org/policy-politics/they-said-no-to-the-federal-school-choice-program-now-3-dems-are-reconsidering/2026/03" target="_blank" ><u>program are reconsidering</u></a>.</p><p>Illinois’ former Invest in Kids program allowed donors to authorized scholarship groups to reduce their state tax burden by as much as $1 million, but state lawmakers allowed that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2024/2/6/24063232/invest-in-kids-private-school-scholarship-program-sunset-illinois-general-assembly" target="_blank" ><u>program to sunset in 2023.</u></a></p><p>The Archdiocese of Chicago has <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2026/01/26/archdiocese-catholic-school-closing-jerome-bruno-richard-francis-borgia-stanislaus-kostka" target="_blank" ><u>attributed declining enrollment and school closures</u></a> to the end of the program, though area Catholic schools have experienced a pattern of shutterings and dwindling student population for decades, due to a combination of factors.</p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: Former Chicago-area nonprofit leader sentenced to year in prison for embezzling $1.9 million
entries.summary: <p>A former Chicago-area nonprofit executive has been sentenced to federal prison for aiding in the embezzlement of about $1.9 million in government grants for after-school programs and poverty aid.</p><p>Barbara Harris, former executive director of the South Holland-based Center for Community Academic Success Partnerships, was sentenced to one year in prison on March 20, according to the U.S. Attorney's Office for the Northern District of Illinois. The 55-year-old South Holland resident pleaded guilty to federal wire fraud last year.</p><p>She, along with fellow executive Tony Bell, of Mattesson, were <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2022/3/1/22957365/center-community-academic-success-partnerships-federal-grants-tony-bell-barbara-harris-government" target="_blank" ><u>indicted in 2022</u></a> after being accused of funneling government grant money intended for after-school enrichment programs to their own bank accounts.</p><p>From 2012 to 2017, the two sent in falsified grant applications that overstated the organization’s projected yearly expenses to the federal 21st Century Community Learning Centers Program, which was then administered by the Illinois State Board of Education, according to court documents. They also allegedly fabricated the names of five subcontractors and opened up false bank accounts for them.</p><p>The pair allegedly used checks to get cash or to purchase money orders; at least $437,000 to pay Bell’s credit card balances and $130,000 to write several checks made payable to an account the two falsely claimed was a subcontractor of the nonprofit, according to court documents.</p><p>In her plea deal, Harris also admitted that, between 2021 to 2023, she was part of a separate fraud scheme in her role as co-executive director of the non-profit South Suburban Community Services. Nearly $99,000 was obtained from the AmeriCorps VISTA program, which awards grants to non-profit organizations working to bring communities out of poverty.</p><p>Bell also pleaded guilty to wire fraud and is set to be sentenced in August, according to the U.S. Attorney’s office.</p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Illinois House advances bill restricting immigrant detention centers in neighborhoods
entries.summary: <p>The Illinois House <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/25/broadview-ice-detention-center-illinois-legislation" >passed a bill </a>Wednesday restricting future immigration detention centers from being built near community buildings. </p><p>The bill would bar the placement of immigration detention centers within 1,500 feet of schools, churches, day care centers, cemeteries, public parks, forest preserves, private residences and public housing. Existing detention facilities like the U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement processing center in Broadview would not be affected by the legislation.</p><p>After passing out of the House along partisan lines with support from Democrats and opposition from Republicans, the bill now goes toe the Illinois Senate. </p><p>House Speaker Emanuel “Chris” Welch, who sponsored the bill and represents areas in and around Broadview, said the legislation is the product of what residents in that community endured during Operation Midway Blitz.</p><p>“What should be a place of peace and routine for that community has too often become a place of fear, disruption, trauma and instability,” Welch said during the bill’s debate. “This bill is not about politics, It's about people.”</p><p>“It's about the little kid at day care who should not grow up around chaos. It's about the family in their home who should not feel like they are living next to a crisis zone. It is about the church member walking into worship who should be met with peace, not tension. It is about saying that in Illinois, we will not place detention facilities in the heart of our neighborhoods and pretend there are no consequences.”</p><p>Republican House Floor Leader Patrick Windhorst, a lawmaker from Metropolis, raised concerns over the legislation's legality and compared it to a California law, struck down in a federal court, that would have phased out private, for-profit prisons and detention centers.</p><p>Welch argued his bill would not meet the same fate, saying the California law was a ban, while this is only a restriction.</p><p>“We are continually picking fights with the federal government,” Windhorst said. “The result of this effort to not work together with the federal government to resolve the issues, particularly related to immigration and enforcement of our laws, has resulted in huge problems in our state.”</p><p>Welch responded, “We're not picking a fight with the federal government, the federal government is picking a fight with us. We have states' rights, we know our rights, we know our power.”</p>
entries.author: Matt Trunfio

Halsted St.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Sandro Miller</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-e0d772c0-3140-11f1-bf8a-33288be89a96"><li><b>“Windfall”</b> is Tarell Alvin McCraney’s drama about a Chicago father who when his child dies in a clash with the police must decide whether to take a cash settlement and forget his grief or remain haunted by memories of the world his child fought to protect. Awoye Timpo directs. From April 9-May 31 at Steppenwolf Theatre, 1650 N. Halsted St. Tickets: $20-$148.50. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://steppenwolf.org" target="_blank" ><u>steppenwolf.org</u></a>.</li><li>For its final production at the North Shore Center before a move in the fall to a new space in downtown Evanston, Northlight Theatre presents <b>“The Angel Next Door,”</b> Paul Slade Smith’s romantic comedy about an author who gathers with screenwriters and his leading lady to adapt his novel for the Broadway stage. From April 9-May 10 at Northlight Theatre, 9501 Skokie Blvd., Skokie. Tickets: $46-$98. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://northlight.org" target="_blank" ><u>northlight.org</u></a>.</li></ul><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-8a0000" name="image-8a0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ca907f6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1333x748+0+626/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4d%2F59%2Feb4517b346678967eb35dcfd07b2%2Fjob-actor-rae-gray-photo-by-ian-mclaren.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/58985b5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1333x748+0+626/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4d%2F59%2Feb4517b346678967eb35dcfd07b2%2Fjob-actor-rae-gray-photo-by-ian-mclaren.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4f3def3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1333x748+0+626/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4d%2F59%2Feb4517b346678967eb35dcfd07b2%2Fjob-actor-rae-gray-photo-by-ian-mclaren.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="JOB actor Rae Gray - photo by Ian McLaren.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4f3def3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1333x748+0+626/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4d%2F59%2Feb4517b346678967eb35dcfd07b2%2Fjob-actor-rae-gray-photo-by-ian-mclaren.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7cfcc9d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1333x748+0+626/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4d%2F59%2Feb4517b346678967eb35dcfd07b2%2Fjob-actor-rae-gray-photo-by-ian-mclaren.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4f3def3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1333x748+0+626/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F4d%2F59%2Feb4517b346678967eb35dcfd07b2%2Fjob-actor-rae-gray-photo-by-ian-mclaren.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Rae Gray stars in “Job,” running from April 9-June 14 at Writers Theatre, 325 Tudor Ct., Glencoe.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ian McLaren</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-e0d772c1-3140-11f1-bf8a-33288be89a96"><li>Rae Gray and Christopher Donahue star in <b>“Job,”</b> Max Wolf Friedlich’s psychological thriller about an employee at a big tech company who must be evaluated by a crisis therapist after video of her screaming breakdown goes viral. David Esbjornson directs. From April 9-June 14 at Writers Theatre, 325 Tudor Ct., Glencoe. Tickets: $50+. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://writerstheatre.org" target="_blank" ><u>writerstheatre.org</u></a>.</li><li>Leslie Buxbaum, David J. Levin and Erin McKeown’s<b> “Out Here”</b> is a new musical about a woman (Becca Ayers) who is caught between what’s been and what’s next as she tries to reimagine her expectations, harmonize with loved ones and trust the process. Chay Yew directs. From April 10-May 10 at Court Theatre, 5535 S. Ellis Ave. Tickets: $60-$90. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://courttheatre.org" target="_blank" ><u>courttheatre.org</u></a>.</li><li>Teatro Vista Productions presents the world premiere of Paloma Nozicka’s <b>“Both,”</b> an examination of family, love, memory and which version of the truth is the one worth believing. Georgette Verdin directs. From April 11-May 10 at Steppenwolf 1700 Theater, 1700 N. Halsted St. Tickets: $47. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://steppenwolf.org" target="_blank" ><u>steppenwolf.org</u></a>.</li><li><b>“Ruthless! The Musical”</b> is the satiric story of an adorably sociopathic 8-year-old who will stop at nothing to land the lead role in her school play. Christina Ramirez directs. From April 15-May 17 at Citadel Theatre, 300 S. Waukegan Rd., Lake Forest. Tickets: $45. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://citadeltheatre.org" target="_blank" ><u>citadeltheatre.org</u></a>.</li><li>Pride Arts presents A.R. Gurney’s <b>“Love Letters”</b> performed as an LGBTQ love story and celebration of lifelong friendships. Jay Españo directs. From April 10-19 at Center on Halsted, 3656 N. Halsted St. Tickets: $25. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://pridearts.org" target="_blank" ><u>pridearts.org</u></a>.</li><li>In Theresa Rebeck’s <b>“Poor Behavior,”</b> a visit from old friends takes an unexpected turn when a bombshell accusation throws niceties out the window. Lauren Katz directs. From April 10-May 10 at Oil Lamp Theater, 1723 Glenview Rd., Glenview. Tickets: $55. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://oillamptheater.org" target="_blank" ><u>oillamptheater.org</u></a>.</li><li>Brightside Theatre presents<b> “Private Lives,”</b> Noel Coward’s comedy about two former lovers who reunite while on their honeymoons with new spouses. Jeffrey Cass directs. From April 10-26 at Meiley-Swallow Hall, North Center College, 31 S. Ellsworth St., Naperville. Tickets: $32-$37. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://brightsidetheatre.com" target="_blank" ><u>brightsidetheatre.com</u></a>.</li><li>Wild Door Theater stages <b>“Maybe Tomorrow,” </b>Max Mondi’s metaphysical puzzle that explores marriage, perception and the unnerving human need to be seen. Andrew Gallant directs. From April 13-May 24 at Athenaeum Center, 2936 N. Southport Ave. Tickets: $29-$43. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://wilddoortheater.com" target="_blank" ><u>wilddoortheater.com</u></a>.</li><li>Jim Steinman and Meat Loaf’s timeless songs are the star of <b>“Bat Out of Hell – The Musical,” </b>a celebration of rock, rebellion and epic love. At 7:30 p.m. April 9 at The Auditorium, 50 E. Ida B. Wells Dr. Tickets: $73+. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://auditoriumtheatre.org" target="_blank" ><u>auditoriumtheatre.org</u></a>.</li><li>Silk Road Cultural Center and Stage Center Theatre present a workshop staging of <b>“Full-On Cleopatra,”</b> Yussef El Guindi’s comedic look at the dramatic finals of one of history’s great romantic couples, Antony and Cleopatra. From April 15-25 at Northeastern Illinois University, Stage Center Theatre, 3701 W. Bryn Mawr Ave. Tickets: $12. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://silkroadculturalcenter.org" target="_blank" ><u>silkroadculturalcenter.org</u></a>.</li><li><b>“Concentration”</b> is Arne Weingart’s theatrical adaptation of his poetry written in the voice of a World War II death camp prisoner. Anna H. Gelman directs. At 7 p.m. April 11 and 2:30 p.m. April 12 at Ruth Page Center for the Arts, 1016 N. Dearborn St. Tickets: $18, $36. Visit <a class="Link" href="http://concentration26.eventbrite.com/" target="_blank" >concentration26.eventbrite.com</a>.</li></ul><h3>Dance</h3><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-760000" name="image-760000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a823150/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1284x721+0+654/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F9b%2F2b%2Fb5598b8349b9a0e87b09663d3898%2Fgirodano-dance-chicago-ids-l-r-eduardo-zambrana-erina-ueda-and-adam-houston-in-a-promo-photo-for-dumb-luck-photo-by-al-blackstone.jpg entries.author: Mary Houlihan

entries.title: Bulls team president Michael Reinsdorf wants boxes checked on next hire
entries.summary: <p>WASHINGTON — The list was sitting right next to Michael Reinsdorf, itching to be read.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls" >Bulls’</a> team president was just waiting for someone to ask.</p><p>As his Tuesday Zoom meeting with the media was winding down, however, he decided to take it upon himself to go ahead and make his feelings known on what he will be looking for in his next choice for basketball operations executive.</p><p>A list that ended up revealing more than just job requirements.</p><p>“I want someone who is process-oriented,” Reinsdorf started off. “I think the biggest mistakes we have made over the years is we have not followed a process.”</p><p>Translation: Fired exec Arturas Karnisovas and general manager Marc Eversley had a plan of no plan. That was evident the last four seasons as they fooled themselves into running back mediocrity after mediocrity.</p><p>“Conviction: We need someone who is not afraid to pull the trigger,” Reinsdorf continued.</p><p>Translation: The Karnisovas regime and the end of the Gar/Pax regime was filled with too many roster spots with players the front office overrated and didn’t want to move off of in time.</p><p>“Communicator: We have a history at the Bulls at the top position other than Pax (John Paxson), they haven’t always been the best communicators going back to the days of Jerry Krause. It wasn’t one of Arturas’ strengths. I want someone who is really strong in communication. Not just internally within the organization. When talking to people like (the media), talking to our fans. I think that is really important in what we are trying to accomplish. What is the plan? Our fans have a right to understand what we are trying to accomplish.”</p><p>No translation needed and as honest as a billionaire owner could be about his shortcomings and the shortcomings of his franchise.</p><p>“We’ve talked about trends, three-point shooting, salary cap increases,” Reinsdorf said. “Back in 2015, 2016, clearly Golden State saw what was coming with the cap and where media (TV deals) were headed and saw ahead of time, ‘Hey, with the new TV deal coming up the cap will increase.’ And they go ahead and made decisions that allowed them to sign guys like Kevin Durant.”</p><p>Translation: The Bulls need to know when to strike and start being ahead of the league rather than chasing it.</p><p>“Talent: One of the things from a business standpoint I’m proud of what I’ve done, I’ve surrounded myself with really smart people. … Whoever takes this role that person one day (will need to have a person under them just as smart).”</p><p>Translation: The days of a Gar Forman (the former GM) pitting people against each other and worrying about his own survival, and a Eversley, who hid in the weeds for far too many years, have ended. The front office needs to collectively be talented and unafraid.</p><p>“Basketball evaluator,” Reinsdorf said. “At the end of the day you have to get the players right.”</p><p>Translation: Marquis Teague should have never been drafted over Draymond Green and Patrick Williams shouldn’t have been selected over Tyrese Haliburton. The Bulls have to stop missing when the obvious stares them in the face. Sometimes a coach knows more than a suit when it comes to talent.</p><p>“Collaboration: The role you have, you have to work across scouting, development departments, analytics; you have to have that alignment,” he said.</p><p>Translation: You can’t always act like the smartest person in the room, talk down to people that don’t agree with the vision, and just flat-out fire people who challenge ideas more than once because they have the data and numbers to prove you’re wrong. Hello Steve Weinman – former Bulls analytics guru who didn’t agree with Karnisovas’ flawed roster building and was inevitably fired for it.</p><p>“Manager: There was a time you could hire a scout to (lead) basketball operations and you’d be fine,” Reinsdorf said. “Basketball operations now is so big. You need to understand how to manage and organize people.”</p><p>Translation: Yes, Karnisovas did build out a player development staff that was almost non-existent under Gar/Pax, but was that group properly managed and heard? Ask shooting guru Peter Patton, who was let go because he spoke his mind on players and their shortcomings. Evaluations that proved to be more right than wrong.</p><p>Was the Reinsdorf presser perfect? No, but it had some real moments that few owners would be willing to pull the curtain back on.</p><p>But the ultimate judge in all of this is what Reinsdorf does next. Otherwise, it was just a list of promises unfulfilled.</p><p></p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" entries.author: Joe Cowley

entries.title: Blackhawks have found power play that works: Connor Bedard quarterbacking five forwards
entries.summary: <p>The <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks" target="_blank" >Blackhawks</a>, in their final four home games, hope to show fans "how good we can be and how good we will be," coach Jeff Blashill said Wednesday.</p><p>That's easier said than done, of course. Two of the Eastern Conference's top four teams by record (Hurricanes and Sabres) and two teams desperately battling for Western playoff spots (Blues and Sharks) will pose difficult tests to close out the Hawks' season.</p><p>The ultra-young Hawks still have a long way to go to convert their talent into victories, too, considering they've won just three of their last 13 games.</p><p>But this late-season <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/03/30/blackhawks-jeff-blashill-experimentation-anton-frondell-power-play" target="_blank" >experimentation time</a> has yielded some interesting and encouraging ideas. That pleases Blashill, who mentioned Monday that "every step forward we take this year means that's one less we have to take a year from now."</p><p>The success of the five-forward power-play unit must headline that list.</p><p>It has improved rapidly every game since Blashill first deployed it five games ago in New Jersey, culminating in one of the Hawks' most impressive power-play performances in years Monday <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/04/07/blackhawks-sharks-new-rival-nhl-future-frank-nazar-power-play" target="_blank" >against the Sharks</a>.</p><p>"They’re definitely catching some confidence and swagger," Blashill said. "[Connor Bedard] up top is real dangerous; he’s a great distributor. [Anton] Frondell on that side is a real weapon, too, with his one-timers. The combo of all five guys, they’re starting to get some chemistry. I think they’ve looked good every time they’ve been together, to be honest."</p><p>Blashill added he would be "crazy not to" consider keeping it intact next season, when everything will matter more as expectations begin to rise.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">GOAL: Frank Nazar caps off another fantastic power play to record his 15th goal of the season. <a href="https://t.co/rCI8eXnJz2">pic.twitter.com/rCI8eXnJz2</a></p>— BHF (@BlackhawksFocus) <a href="https://twitter.com/BlackhawksFocus/status/2041368398341313020?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 7, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>The first-instinct concern about having no defensemen on the ice is defending shorthanded chances by the opposing penalty kill, but that hasn't been a problem so far.</p><p>Having a forward with Teuvo Teravainen's defensive skills on the ice mitigates that weakness. The unit has allowed only two shorthanded scoring chances and zero goals in about 17 minutes together.</p><p>On the other hand, they've produced about 1.85 scoring chances per minute — up from a paltry 0.87 <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/02/01/blackhawks-power-play-practice-connor-bedard-sam-rinzel-penalty-kill" target="_blank" >previously this season</a> — and scored basically four goals (one of which occurred seconds after a penalty expired) in those same 17 minutes.</p><p>Retrieving cleared pucks and executing offensive-zone entries seemed like another potential weakness at first, but Bedard and Frank Nazar appear perfectly comfortable doing that and making drop-passes to each other — or using their speed and elusiveness to enter the zone themselves.</p><p>And once inside the zone, Bedard has proven adept at quarterbacking plays from the blue line, making accurate passes or shooting and getting the puck through screens.</p><p>"He's moving the puck so well, and when he's moving over to me, it's right where I want it for the shot," Frondell said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">What a sequence from the top power-play unit. Chance after chance: <a href="https://t.co/Bsu2ZB9ZXO">pic.twitter.com/Bsu2ZB9ZXO</a></p>— BHF (@BlackhawksFocus) <a href="https://twitter.com/BlackhawksFocus/status/2041363695553368488?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 7, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>Blocked shots and intercepted passes are most important things to avoid, since even missed shots can create chaos in a good way. </p><p>And Bedard, who has found it's "100 times easier to shoot a puck from up there," has only gotten three of his 10 shot attempts blocked. Defensemen Sam Rinzel and Artyom Levshunov were getting 46% and 44% of their attempts blocked, respectively.</p><p>Bedard also frequently leaves the blue line to roam around the zone, which keeps the entire unit in motion and continuously opens up new passing and shooting lanes.</p><p>Both Frondell and Nazar can now play simultaneously on the flanks, which creates the Frondell one-timer threat that Blashill mentioned and makes better use of Nazar's playmaking skills than the bumper role did.</p><p>Tyler Bertuzzi's job down low, where he's extremely effective, hasn't changed. And with both him and Frondell on the unit, the Hawks have two guys who excel at winning pucks back, which is another plus.</p><p>The unit will have to demonstrate again in training camp next season that its unusual concoction still works, but they've at least earned the opportunity to start it together.</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH" data-module data-hide-authors="true" entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Lincoln Park Zoo adopts two young Sichuan takin brothers
entries.summary: <p>Two young members of the goat-antelope family are making their new home at the Lincoln Park Zoo.</p><p>The Sichuan takins — brothers Porter, 3, and Taiyang, 2 — are a species native to China. But the pair were born about 100 miles away from Chicago at the Potawatomi Zoo in South Bend, Indiana.</p><p>In their new home, the takins will show off their curved horns that look like a handlebar mustache, and loose flap of skin under their chins, known as a dewlap.</p><p>“Takins are such a fun and unique animal to see, and we’re excited to welcome these two brothers to their new home here at Lincoln Park Zoo,” curator Dan Boehm said in the news release announcing the brothers' arrival. “We’re quickly getting to know their personalities and have been impressed already with how quickly they’ve settled into their habitat.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c10000" name="image-c10000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275"
